1.18: ho logos ho tou staurou (ho logos ho tou staurou, 'the word of the cross'): Paul's phrase is deliberate — not 'the theology of the cross' or 'the doctrine of the atonement' but the logos itself, the very message. moria (mōria, foolishness): Greco-Roman rhetoric valued wisdom (sophia) displayed through elegant argumentation (logos). Execution on a cross (stauros) was the most shameful death possible — reserved for slaves, pirates, and enemies of the state (Cicero Pro Rabirio 5.16: 'let the very word cross be far from the body, eyes, and ears of Roman citizens'). That this is the power (dynamis) of God for those being saved is Paul's inversion of all Greco-Roman honor-shame discourse.
1.24: Christon Theou dynamin kai Theou sophian (Christon Theou dynamin kai Theou sophian): 'Christ the power of God and the wisdom of God.' Paul here makes Christ the personification of both divine attributes — power and wisdom are not merely Christ's qualities but identified with his person. This comes after the Corinthian division over rhetorical wisdom (1:12: some follow Apollo's eloquence, some Peter, some Paul). Paul's response: the divided parties are competing over what is no competition — Christ alone is Wisdom, and that wisdom is cruciform.
1.30: sophias kai dikaiosynes kai hagiasmos kai apolytrosis: 'wisdom, righteousness, sanctification, and redemption.' The four nouns in asyndeton give Christ four forensic/cultic roles. Dikaiosyne (righteousness) covers the forensic standing; hagiasmos (sanctification/holiness) the cultic status; apolytrosis (redemption/release) the commercial-legal deliverance from bondage. That these are all said to be 'from God' (apo theou) makes clear they are received, not achieved. Paul is attacking the Corinthians' pride in human wisdom by showing that what they need — status, holiness, freedom — comes only from Christ.
1.1: κλητὸς ἀπόστολος (klētos apostolos) — called apostle. The adjective klētos (called, invited) places Paul's apostleship entirely in the divine initiative: he did not choose the role; he was called into it. The same word describes the Corinthians in v.2 (klētois hagiois — called saints). Paul's apostolic authority and the community's identity rest on the same ground: divine calling, not human qualification.
1.2: ἡγιασμένοις (hēgiasmenois) — perfect passive participle: those who have been sanctified and remain in that sanctified state. The perfect tense is not merely historical; it describes an ongoing condition that results from a past completed action. The community is already sanctified — a state given in Christ — before any ethical transformation is discussed. This established holiness is then the basis for the ethical demands that follow in the letter.
1.3: χάρις καὶ εἰρήνη (charis kai eirēnē) — grace and peace. The standard Pauline greeting combines the Greek epistolary greeting (chairein, greetings/rejoice, modified to charis) with the Jewish shalom. The combination is not merely stylistic; charis names the mode of divine action (unmerited favor) and eirēnē names the resulting state (covenant wholeness). Both flow "from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ" — a coordination of sources that implicitly equates Jesus with the Father as a source of divine blessing.
1.4: εὐχαριστῶ (eucharistō) — present tense: I am always (pantote) thanking my God. The continuous present in a thanksgiving formula was a conventional letter-opener, but Paul's formula is theologically dense: the thanksgiving is specifically "because of the grace of God given you in Christ Jesus." The gift is not Paul's teaching or their response but God's prior grace.
1.5: λόγῳ... γνώσει (in all speech and all knowledge) — both nouns are datives of sphere (enriched in the sphere of speech; enriched in the sphere of knowledge). The very gifts Paul will challenge (rhetorical skill, knowledge) are acknowledged as real gifts from God. The problem is not the gifts themselves but the Corinthians' misunderstanding of their source and purpose.
1.6: τὸ μαρτύριον τοῦ Χριστοῦ ἐβεβαιώθη (the testimony of Christ was confirmed/made firm) — aorist passive: a completed confirming act. The verb bebaioō is a legal term for formally ratifying or guaranteeing a contract or legal status. God's confirming of the gospel testimony in the community (through the charismatic gifts of vv.5-7) is a legal ratification, not merely experiential validation.
1.7: ἀπεκδεχομένους (apekdechomenous) — present active participle: those who are eagerly awaiting. The prefix apo-ek- intensifies the waiting: earnest, sustained expectation. The community is defined by eschatological posture: they are "waiting" people. The apokalypsis (revelation/unveiling) they await is Christ's definitive disclosure at the Parousia.
1.8: βεβαιώσει (bebaiōsei) — future indicative: he will confirm/make firm. The same root as v.6 (bebaioō): the God who confirmed the testimony in the past (v.6) will also confirm the community at the end. The blameless (anenkletous) state is not ethical achievement but the forensic verdict of the judge — they will be found without charge before the tribunal.
1.9: πιστὸς ὁ θεός (pistos ho theos) — God is faithful. The adjective pistos shares its root with pistis (faith/trust); YHWH's faithfulness is his character of covenant reliability that makes trust in him possible. The calling was through God's faithfulness (not Corinthian deserving), and the sustaining to the end will be through the same faithfulness. The entire argument for the community's security rests not on their spiritual status but on God's character.
1.10: παρακαλῶ (parakalō) — I appeal, I urge. The present tense marks an urgent ongoing appeal; this is not a polite request but the technical language of pastoral exhortation. The root para-kaleō (calling alongside) is also the root of paraklētos (advocate, helper). The appeal is σχίσματα (schismata — tears, splits): Paul uses the language of fabric being torn. The community is not merely divided in opinion; it is being structurally ripped apart.
1.11: ἐριδες (erides) — contentions, rivalries. The plural of eris (strife personified as a goddess in Greek tradition); the term describes the kind of passionate competitive conflict that characterizes rhetoric competitions and legal disputes. The Corinthians' factional disputes carry the energy of public rhetorical combat.
1.12: ἐγὼ δέ εἰμι (I am of...) — the emphatic personal pronoun egō gives each faction member a proud, assertive voice: I belong to Paul, I belong to Apollos. The formula echoes the Hellenistic philosophical school-allegiance language ("I am of Plato's school"). The Corinthians are treating the gospel message as a philosophical school with competing teachers, each school having a distinct character to which adherents belong.
1.13: μεμέρισται ὁ Χριστός (has Christ been divided?) — perfect passive indicative used interrogatively with expected negative answer. The perfect tense is pointed: has Christ been and-remained-in-a-state-of-division? The absurdity Paul highlights is theological: the divided community implies a divided Christ, which is impossible. The factions have made an ontological claim about Christ's unity without realizing it.
1.14: Κρίσπον καὶ Γάϊον (Crispus and Gaius) — Paul recalls the names of those he personally baptized from memory, noting his relief that he baptized so few. Crispus is likely the synagogue ruler of Acts 18:8; Gaius is mentioned in Rom 16:23. The parenthetical quality of this verse shows Paul working through his argument without a prepared text.
1.15: ἐβαπτίσθητε (you were baptized) — aorist passive: the single completed event of baptism. Paul's concern is the preposition: baptized into whose name? Baptism in the name of someone meant initiation into that person's sphere of authority and identity (cf. baptism into Moses in 10:2). Being baptized into Paul's name would have been a catastrophic misrepresentation of whose people the Corinthians were.
1.16: The parenthetical aside about Stephanas's household — "beyond that I don't know if I baptized anyone else" — is a rare glimpse of Paul composing in real time, correcting his statement as he writes. It demonstrates that the letter is not a polished rhetorical piece but genuine correspondence. The spontaneous correction also makes the theological argument that follows more credible: Paul is not trying to disclaim baptizing people; it simply was not his primary mission.
1.17: οὐκ ἐν σοφίᾳ λόγου (not in wisdom of speech) — the genitive lōgou (of word/speech) specifies: not wisdom in the abstract but wisdom-as-rhetorical-skill. Sophia logou was the technical term for the rhetorical eloquence of the Sophists — persuasion through elegant speech. Paul's mission was to preach euangelion in such a way that the cross would not be emptied of its power (kenōthē, aorist passive subjunctive: lest it be emptied).
1.19: γέγραπται γάρ (for it stands written) — perfect passive: the standard citation formula, using the perfect to indicate that the written word stands in permanent force. The text cited (Isa 29:14) is not merely a historical statement; it is a permanent divine declaration that remains in effect. The perfect gegraptai is how Paul marks the ongoing authority of all his scriptural citations.
1.20: ποῦ σοφός (where is the wise person?) — the triple rhetorical question (pou... pou... pou) is a rhetorical taunt. Γραμματεύς (grammateus = scribe, secretary, teacher of the law) locates the wisdom-failure in the Jewish scribal tradition specifically; συζητητής τοῦ αἰῶνος τούτου (debater of this age) locates it in Hellenistic philosophical disputation. Both are exposed as futile by the cross. The phrase ὁ αἰὼν οὗτος (this age) introduces the apocalyptic two-age schema.
1.21: εὐδόκησεν ὁ θεὸς (God was pleased, God decided) — aorist active: God made a single decisive choice. The verb eudokeō connotes both pleasure and purposeful decision. God's sovereign decision to save through the preaching that the world considers foolish (mōria tou kērygmatos — the foolishness OF the proclamation, not a foolish proclamation) is the ground for the cross-strategy. The genitive expresses the content that renders the proclamation foolish in human estimation.
1.22: σημεῖα αἰτοῦσιν... σοφίαν ζητοῦσιν (demand signs... seek wisdom) — the verbs carry distinct tones: aiteō (demand, request with expectation of receiving) vs. zēteō (seek, investigate). Jews demand visible divine attestation; Greeks pursue intellectual coherence. Both are seeking a gospel that fits their existing criteria for what divine action must look like.
1.23:
Χριστὸν ἐσταυρωμένον (Christ crucified) — the perfect passive participle estaurōmenon is crucial: not "Christ who was crucified" (aorist) but "Christ who has been crucified and remains in the crucified state." The crucifixion is not merely a past event in Christ's biography; it is his permanent defining identity. The Messiah is permanently defined by the cross.

σκάνδαλον (skandalon = stumbling block, trap) — more than offense; the word denotes a mechanism that causes someone to fall. The cross is not merely culturally offensive to Jewish ears but structurally incompatible with Jewish messianic expectation of a conquering king.
1.25: τὸ μωρὸν τοῦ θεοῦ... τὸ ἀσθενὲς τοῦ θεοῦ (the foolish of God, the weak of God) — these are neuter substantives, not adjectival descriptions of specific things but abstractions: "the-foolish-thing-that-is-God's" and "the-weak-thing-that-is-God's." The genitive identifies these as belonging to God's mode of action, not as genuine attributes. What appears as foolishness in God's action surpasses all human wisdom in its effectiveness.
1.26: Βλέπετε γὰρ τὴν κλῆσιν ὑμῶν (consider your calling) — the imperative blepete (look at, consider) directs attention to the empirical evidence: the community itself is the argument. Klēsis (calling, vocation) is the same word-group as the klētos of v.1; the Corinthians' own existence as a called community proves the point about divine selection criteria.
1.27: ἐξελέξατο (exelexato) — aorist middle: God chose for himself. The middle voice emphasizes the divine self-involvement in the act of election; God chose these for his own purposes. The threefold repetition (exelexato... exelexato... exelexato) creates an anaphora that insists on divine election as the exclusive explanation for the community's existence.
1.28: τὰ μὴ ὄντα (the things that are not) — present participle of eimi with the negative: literally "the non-beings." Paul's language touches on creation-from-nothing; the God who calls things-that-are-not into existence (Rom 4:17) is the same God who calls the social nobodies of Corinth into his purpose. The community's existence is a creational act.
1.29: ὅπως μὴ καυχήσηται πᾶσα σάρξ (so that no flesh might boast) — purpose clause with aorist subjunctive: the specific aim of the divine selection strategy. Pasa sarx (all flesh) is a Hebrew-Bible expression (kol basar) denoting humanity as mortal, creaturely, limited — the opposite of the divine. The prohibition on boasting before God is absolute: no creaturely ground for pride before the Creator.
1.31: ὁ καυχώμενος ἐν κυρίῳ καυχάσθω (let the one who boasts boast in the Lord) — the present imperative kauchāsthō: an ongoing rule, not a single act. The only legitimate boasting (kauchēsis) is in the Lord — specifically, in knowing YHWH who exercises covenant-love, justice, and righteousness (Jer 9:24). Paul's citation replaces all forms of human boasting (wisdom, skill, status, spiritual gifts) with a single legitimate form: covenant-knowledge of the God who acts in the cross.

2.2: ou gar ekrina ti eidenai en hymin ei me Iesoun Christon kai touton estauromemon: 'For I decided to know nothing among you except Jesus Christ and him crucified.' Ekrina is deliberate resolve, not ignorance. Paul came to the most philosophically sophisticated city in Greece and chose (krinō) to deploy only one argument: the cross. Estauromenos (crucified) is the perfect passive participle — not 'who was crucified' (past and done) but 'who is the crucified one' (ongoing identity). Paul's Christology is permanently cross-shaped.
2.13: pneumatikois pneumatika synkrinontes (pneumatikois pneumatika synkrinotes): 'interpreting spiritual things to spiritual people' (or 'comparing spiritual things with spiritual things'). The phrase is syntactically ambiguous: synkrinō can mean 'combine' (matching Spirit-words with Spirit-people) or 'interpret' (explaining spiritual realities). Either way, Paul's point is that the Spirit's deep things (v. 10: ta bathe tou theou) are disclosed only within the Spirit's own interpretive community — those who have the mind of Christ (v. 16).
2.1: κατὰ ὑπεροχὴν λόγου ἢ σοφίας (with superiority of speech or wisdom) — the prepositional phrase kata hyperochen (according to pre-eminence/superiority) describes the rhetorical mode Paul deliberately did not use. Some manuscripts read μυστήριον (mystery) for μαρτύριον (testimony) — if mystērion is original, it introduces the hidden-wisdom theme from the start; if martyrion, the focus is on testimony/proclamation. The UBS/NA reading martyrion is followed here, but the variant is ancient and substantive.
2.3: ἐγὼ ἐν ἀσθενείᾳ καὶ ἐν φόβῳ καὶ ἐν τρόμῳ πολλῷ (I in weakness and in fear and in much trembling) — the triple prepositional phrase (en... kai en... kai en...) with emphatic pollō (much) describes Paul's actual experiential state in Corinth. This is not false modesty; it is the self-conscious acknowledgment that the divine power operates through a broken vessel. The contrast with the Corinthian puffed-up wisdom sets the apostolic paradigm: weakness, not strength, is the proper medium for divine power (cf. 2 Cor 12:9-10).
2.4: ἀπόδειξις πνεύματος καὶ δυνάμεως (demonstration of the Spirit and power) — apodeixis is a technical rhetorical/philosophical term for logical demonstration or proof, the strongest form of argument. Paul claims the Spirit provides a different kind of apodeixis than rhetoric does — not logical persuasion but direct conviction through the Spirit's power. The genitive pneumatos (of the Spirit) makes the Spirit the mode and content of this demonstration, not its audience.
2.5: ἵνα ἡ πίστις ὑμῶν μὴ ᾖ ἐν σοφίᾳ ἀνθρώπων ἀλλ᾽ ἐν δυνάμει θεοῦ (so that your faith might not be in human wisdom but in God's power) — purpose clause with present subjunctive: the ongoing character of the faith-stance matters, not just its origin. Faith resting on rhetorical persuasion would be as fragile as the arguer's skill; faith resting on God's power rests on something that cannot be refuted by a better rhetorician.
2.6: τελείοις (teleiois = the mature, the complete) — the word was used in Hellenistic mystery religions for the fully initiated, and in philosophical schools for the advanced student. Paul's use is deliberately counter-cultural: he does speak wisdom, but to the teleioi; the irony is that the Corinthians who claim this status are proved by their divisions to be nēpioi (infants, 3:1). The wisdom Paul offers the mature is NOT the wisdom of this age.
2.7: ἐν μυστηρίῳ τὴν ἀποκεκρυμμένην (in mystery, the having-been-hidden-one) — the perfect passive participle apokekrimmēnēn (from apokryptō, to hide thoroughly) emphasizes the completeness of the prior hiddenness. This wisdom was not merely obscure but thoroughly concealed before God revealed it. The perfect tense insists that the prior hiddenness is the necessary context for understanding the magnitude of the revelation.
2.8: τὸν κύριον τῆς δόξης (the Lord of glory) — an unusual christological title. In the LXX and Jewish apocalyptic literature (1 Enoch 22:14; 25:3; 27:3; 36:4; Ps 24:7-10), Kyrios tēs doxēs is a title for YHWH as the glorious king of the cosmos. Paul applies it to Jesus as the one the rulers of this age crucified — an implicit argument for divine identity. The rulers' ignorance of whom they crucified is matched by the title's claim about who he was.
2.9: ἃ ὀφθαλμὸς οὐκ εἶδεν (what eye has not seen) — Paul introduces a free citation of Isa 64:4 and possibly 65:17 with alla kathōs gegraptai (but as it stands written). The emphasis is on the radical incommensurability of God's prepared blessings with any human investigative framework: not seen, not heard, not risen up into the heart of a human being. The three negated faculties (sight, hearing, understanding) exhaust the channels of normal human cognition.
2.10: ἡμῖν δὲ ἀπεκάλυψεν ὁ θεὸς διὰ τοῦ πνεύματος (but to us God revealed through the Spirit) — aorist active: a completed divine disclosure. Πνεῦμα (pneuma) here carries its highest dispute load (G4151, dispute_level 3): it is Spirit (divine power) not merely wind, and specifically the Holy Spirit as an agent of revelation, not merely an inspirational force. Eraunaō (searches) in the present tense: the Spirit's investigation of the depths of God (ta bathē tou theou) is ongoing and penetrating.
2.11: τίς γὰρ οἶδεν ἀνθρώπων τὰ τοῦ ἀνθρώπου (for who of people knows the things of a person) — the analogy moves from the human level to the divine: just as only a person's own spirit knows that person's inner thoughts, so only God's Spirit knows God's depths. The analogy grounds the Spirit's cognitive access to the divine mind in a recognizable epistemology. Πνεῦμα used twice: human spirit (the person's own consciousness) and the Spirit of God — different referents in parallel positions.
2.12: ἐλάβομεν (we received) — aorist: a completed act of reception. The Spirit received is specifically οὐ τὸ πνεῦμα τοῦ κόσμου (not the spirit of the world) — the articular noun construction (the spirit of the world vs. the Spirit from God) makes these two definite, distinct realities. Ἵνα εἰδῶμεν (so that we might know) — purpose: Spirit-reception has a specific cognitive goal: knowing what God has freely given, not experiencing ecstasy or demonstrating power.
2.14: ψυχικὸς ἄνθρωπος (the natural/soulish person) — psychikos is the adjective from psychē (soul, the animating life-principle). The natural person operates at the level of the psychē alone, without the Spirit. Paul's anthropological categories are not body/soul/spirit in a Platonic hierarchy but rather Spirit-equipped vs. Spirit-lacking — a pneumatological distinction, not a metaphysical one. Μωρία (foolishness) recurs from 1:18-25: the natural mind's verdict on Spirit-matters is the same as the cross-rejector's verdict on the gospel.
2.15: ὁ δὲ πνευματικὸς ἀνακρίνει μὲν πάντα (the spiritual person discerns all things) — anakrinō is a legal-interrogative term: to examine, to cross-examine, to evaluate after investigation. The spiritual person has the capacity to subject all things to discerning examination — not omniscience but Spirit-enabled discernment. The passive counterpart: autos de hyp' oudenos anakrinetai — but he himself is examined by no one (oudeis = no human authority can render a binding verdict on him, since his life is accountable to God alone).
2.16: νοῦν κυρίου (mind of the Lord) — Paul cites Isa 40:13 (LXX: tis egnō noun kyriou? — who knew the mind of the Lord?) to establish that unequipped humanity cannot know God's mind. Then the jarring counter: ἡμεῖς δὲ νοῦν Χριστοῦ ἔχομεν (but we have the mind of Christ). The substitution of Christou (Christ's) for kyriou (Lord's) is a divine-identity claim: having the mind of Christ is having the mind of YHWH. This is the climax of the entire wisdom-Spirit argument of chapters 1-2.

3.16: naos theou este kai to pneuma tou theou oikei en hymin (naos theou este kai to pneuma tou theou oikei en hymin): 'you are the temple of God and the Spirit of God dwells in you.' Naos is the inner sanctuary (the Most Holy Place and Holy Place), not merely the outer court (hieron). Paul uses the corporate 'you' (plural in Greek) — the community together is the naos, the place of divine habitation. This radically relocates the dwelling of God: not in the Jerusalem temple but in the community gathered around Christ. The destruction of this community-temple by division and immorality (v. 17) is therefore as serious as desecrating the Jerusalem sanctuary.
3.1: σαρκίνοις (sarkinois = made of flesh) — the adjective describes constitution, not conduct: Paul addresses them as people who are, by constitution, fleshly (infants in Christ). The contrast in v.3 shifts to σαρκικοί (sarkikoi = flesh-dominated, acting according to flesh) — now a description of chosen conduct. The move from what they were (understandably fleshly as new believers) to what they still are (inexcusably flesh-dominated given time passed) is the accusatory pivot of this section.
3.2: γάλα ὑμᾶς ἐπότισα, οὐ βρῶμα (I fed you milk, not solid food) — the feeding-metaphor distinguishes elementary instruction (milk) from mature teaching (broma = solid food). Epotisa (aorist: I gave to drink) is the technical word for providing drink to someone, used also for watering plants (v.6, 7-8). The metaphor fuses parental feeding and agricultural irrigation in a single letter-unit.
3.3: σαρκικοί ἐστε (you are fleshly) — the present tense is judgmental: this is their current state. Zēlos kai eris (jealousy and strife) are the presenting symptoms of fleshly operation. The rhetorical question ouchi sarkikoi este kai kata anthrōpon peripatete? (are you not fleshly and are you not walking according to the merely human standard?) uses the Pauline kata anthrōpon (according to the human) as the mark of operating below the Spirit's level.
3.4: ἄνθρωποί ἐστε (you are merely human beings) — the demonstrative is dismissive: mere humans, operating at the merely human level. The factions are identified as the symptom; the underlying disease is operating at the human level rather than the Spirit level. Paul does not say they are not Christians but that they are functioning like people who have no Spirit.
3.5: διάκονοι (diakonoi = servants, deacons) — the lowest service category, distinct from the master or the overseer. Paul and Apollos are not party leaders but ministerial workers through whom faith came. Ὡς ὁ κύριος ἔδωκεν ἑκάστῳ (as the Lord gave to each one) — aorist, a completed assignment. The diversity of ministry is divinely assigned distribution, not competitive differentiation.
3.6: ἐγὼ ἐφύτευσα, Ἀπολλῶς ἐπότισεν (I planted, Apollos watered) — both verbs are aorist: completed historical acts with specific referents. The imperfect ηὔξανεν (was causing to grow) for God's action stands in contrast: not a single act but the continuous, ongoing, sustaining growth that only God provides. The tense contrast is theologically deliberate: human ministry is momentary; divine growth is continuous.
3.7: οὔτε ὁ φυτεύων ἐστίν τι οὔτε ὁ ποτίζων (neither the planter nor the waterer is anything) — the negation of being (is anything) radically de-centers the human worker. The present tense denotes the permanent status: the planter's identity as nothing-in-themselves is ongoing, not just situational. Only God who causes growth is anything, and he remains the subject of the imperfect auxanōn (the one growing).
3.8: ὁ φυτεύων δὲ καὶ ὁ ποτίζων ἕν εἰσιν (the one who plants and the one who waters are one) — not one person but one in status and direction. They will each receive their own reward (idion misthon) according to their own labor (idion kopon): individual accountability to God, not competitive comparison with each other.
3.9: θεοῦ γεώργιον, θεοῦ οἰκοδομή ἐστε (you are God's field, God's building) — the genitives are emphatic by fronting: God's field, God's building — not Paul's, not Apollos's. The double metaphor (agricultural → architectural) shifts the imagery for the section that follows. The community belongs to God; the builders are stewards, not owners.
3.10: ἀρχιτέκτων (architekton = master builder, architect) — the root of our English word. Paul lays the foundation with the skill of a master craftsman. Ἄλλος δὲ ἐποικοδομεῖ (another builds on it) — present tense: ongoing building activity of others. The warning hekastos blepetō pōs epoikodomei (let each one take care how he builds) is present imperative: a rule for ongoing building practice.
3.11: θεμέλιον γὰρ ἄλλον οὐδεὶς δύναται θεῖναι (for no one is able to lay another foundation) — the double negation (allon = other/different + oudeis dunatai = no one is able) is absolute: the impossibility is not practical but ontological. Christ is not one available foundation among others but the only possible one. Ὃς ἔστιν Ἰησοῦς Χριστός (which is Jesus Christ) — the relative clause identifies the foundation with the full messianic name.
3.12: χρυσόν, ἄργυρον, λίθους τιμίους, ξύλον, χόρτον, καλάμην (gold, silver, precious stones, wood, hay, straw) — the three durable materials (gold, silver, precious stones) and three combustible materials (wood, hay, straw) are not identified with specific teachings or errors. The test will reveal which category each building component belongs to; speculative identification of what each material represents misses the point — the test is eschatological, not hermeneutical.
3.13: ἡ ἡμέρα (the Day) — the definite article marks this as the eschatological Day of the Lord; Paul's readers needed no explanation of which Day. Ἐν πυρὶ ἀποκαλύπτεται (it will be revealed through fire) — the fire is not punitive first but revelatory: the Day's fire reveals what kind of work it is. The testing-metaphor draws on Mal 3:2-3 (the refiner's fire). The distinction between the builder's work being burned (katakahēsetai) and the builder being saved (sōthēsetai) is a crucial soteriological point: salvation is not based on the quality of the ministerial work.
3.14: εἰ τινος τὸ ἔργον μενεῖ ὃ ἐπῳκοδόμησεν, μισθὸν λήψεται (if anyone's work which they built remains, they will receive a reward) — first-class conditional (if, and it does): those whose work survives the eschatological fire will receive reward. Misthon (reward) is the standard term for wages due for work performed — eschatological recompense, not salvation itself.
3.15: αὐτὸς δὲ σωθήσεται, οὕτως δὲ ὡς διὰ πυρός (but he himself will be saved, but as through fire) — the famous "saved, but so as through fire" clause. The builder whose work is consumed will be saved anyway — but with the experience of losing everything one built. The construction hōs dia pyros (as if through fire) is an adverbial comparison: the manner of salvation is like escaping a burning building with nothing but one's life.
3.17: φθερεῖ αὐτόν (will destroy him) — future indicative: God's judgment on those who destroy the community is certain and proportionate: destroyer destroyed. The chiastic structure: if anyone destroys God's temple / God will destroy that person / for God's temple is sacred / which [temple] you are — the chiasm identifies the community as the sacred object of divine protection.
3.18: μηδεὶς ἑαυτὸν ἐξαπατάτω (let no one deceive himself) — present imperative with mēdeis (no one): an absolute prohibition. Exapataō (deceive thoroughly, from ex-apataō) intensifies the deception warning. The condition for gaining the wisdom of this age is becoming mōros (a fool) by the standards of that same age — the cross-pattern applied to epistemology.
3.19: δρασσόμενος (drassomenos = catching, gripping) — a vivid present participle from drassomai: to grab, to seize. The image is of divine hands actively catching the wise in mid-cunning. Panourgia (craftiness, cunning) is a loaded word: pan + ergon (all-deed) = willing to do anything, totally unprincipled cleverness. The same word describes the serpent's approach to Eve (2 Cor 11:3; Gen 3:1 LXX).
3.20: διαλογισμοὶ τῶν σοφῶν (reasonings of the wise) — the noun dialogismoi (reasonings, debates, inner calculations) carries a slightly negative charge in Paul: the inner calculations of people relying on their own rational framework. Mataios (futile, empty, worthless) — the word used in the LXX for hebel (vanity/vapor in Ecclesiastes) and for idol-worship as empty/futile activity.
3.21: ὥστε μηδεὶς καυχάσθω ἐν ἀνθρώποις (so then let no one boast in human beings) — the present imperative kauchāsthō again: no ongoing boasting in human leaders. The counter-declaration panta gar hymōn estin (for all things are yours) is the positive: instead of the community belonging to the leaders, the leaders belong to the community. The ownership is reversed.
3.22: Παῦλος ἢ Ἀπολλῶς ἢ Κηφᾶς — the three human leaders who served as the faction-labels are listed as among the things that belong to the community, along with kosmos, zōē, thanatos, ta enestōta, ta mellonta (world, life, death, the present, the future). The cosmic scope of the community's inheritance makes factional boasting about any single leader absurd: the community that possesses the world should not divide over who preaches best.
3.23: ὑμεῖς δὲ Χριστοῦ, Χριστὸς δὲ θεοῦ (you are of Christ, and Christ is of God) — the chain of possession concludes. The community belongs to Christ (not to Paul, Apollos, or Cephas); Christ belongs to God (not as an independent source of salvation but as the Son who executes the Father's will). The chain grounds all authority in the Father and establishes Christ as the only legitimate head of the community.

6.19: to soma hymon naos tou en hymin hagiou pneumatos estin (to sōma hymōn naos tou en hymin hagiou pneumatos estin): 'your body is a temple of the Holy Spirit within you.' Here the body (sōma) is singular — each believer's individual body is a sanctuary of the Spirit. This shifts 3:16's corporate sanctuary-language to the individual person. The implication for sexual ethics (vv. 15-18: not prostitution) is that bodily sin is sanctuary-desecration — the fornication charge is framed not as moral failure but as spatial-theological violation of the Spirit's dwelling place. Agorazō (to purchase, v. 20) is commercial-legal redemption language: you were bought at a price, therefore you are not your own property to dispose of as you wish.
6.1: tolmai (dare) — the verb implies moral effrontery, not merely courage. The act requires a kind of audacity. epi tōn adikōn (before the unrighteous) — adikoi here is not a generic moral epithet but a technical category: those outside the covenant community, who by definition lack the standard of righteousness that would qualify them to adjudicate covenant disputes. epi tōn hagiōn (before the saints) — the church as the community of the holy, the proper forum for internal disputes.
6.2: ouk oidate hoti hoi hagioi ton kosmon krienousi (do you not know that the saints will judge the world?) — the ouk oidate (do you not know) formula recurs eight times in 1 Corinthians (6:2, 3, 9, 15, 16, 19; 5:6; 3:16) — Paul appeals to a body of shared knowledge the Corinthians already possess but are not applying. The saints judging the world is a Second Temple expectation (Dan 7:22; Wis 3:7-8; 1 En. 1:9): the redeemed community participates in eschatological judgment. If they will judge the cosmos, they are surely competent for petty disputes (kritēria elachistōn, smallest cases).
6.3: angelous krienoumen (we will judge angels) — not a speculative assertion but a shared eschatological belief. Paul assumes his readers accept this; he appeals to it to shame their failure at the lower task of judging this-life matters (biōtika, things of this life).
6.4: exouthenēmenous en tē ekklēsia (those who are held in contempt in the church) — either ironic (Paul is sarcastically suggesting they are appointing the scorned as judges) or a genuine re-evaluation: those the world scorns are qualified by their standing in the community. The sentence is interrogative or imperatival — scholars debate whether Paul is mocking their behavior or directing it. Either reading surfaces the absurdity of taking Christian disputes before pagan courts.
6.5: pros entropēn (to shame you) — Paul invokes the honor-shame framework directly. sophos (wise person): the vocabulary of wisdom picks up Paul's sustained argument from chs. 1–4. The Corinthians pride themselves on wisdom (sophia); Paul now says their legal behavior proves there is not one person wise enough to resolve a dispute between siblings in Christ. This is a deliberate inversion of their self-image.
6.6: adelphos meta adelphou krinetai (brother goes to court against brother) — the repetition of adelphos (brother) intensifies the contradiction: the covenant-bond of siblinghood is violated by the very act of external litigation. kai touto epi apistoōn (and this before unbelievers) — the public visibility makes it a witness-failure, not only a community-failure.
6.7: hēttēma (defeat, loss): a legal-commercial term; already, before the verdict is rendered, the very existence of the lawsuit is a defeat. The Stoic/Cynic philosophical tradition valued being wronged without retaliation as the mark of the sage; Paul invokes this expectation — ouchi mallon adikeisthe? ouchi mallon apostereisthe? (why not rather be wronged? why not rather be cheated?) — but grounds it not in philosophical virtue but in community honor and eschatological perspective.
6.8: alla hymeis adikeite kai apostereite (instead, you yourselves do wrong and cheat) — the reversal: the Corinthians are not merely failing to endure wrong; they are actively perpetrating it against brothers and sisters. Apostereō (defraud) is the same verb used in 7:5 for depriving a spouse — here it is economic exploitation within the community.
6.9: adikoi (the unrighteous) connects to v. 1's epi tōn adikōn: Paul now defines the category from the inside. The vice catalogue that follows is one of Paul's four (others: Rom 1:29-31; Gal 5:19-21; Col 3:5-8). malakoi (malakoi, lit. soft ones): used in secular Greek for the passive partner in male same-sex acts; also for men who adopted feminine roles. arsenokoitai (arsenokoitai): a Pauline coinage (earliest known use); formed from arsēn (male) + koitē (bed/sexual intercourse) — the compound reflects the LXX of Lev 18:22 (meta arsenos koitēn gynaikos) and 20:13 (hos an koimēthē meta arsenos koitēn gynaikos). Paul constructed the term from the Torah text that prohibits the act; it denotes the active male partner.
6.10: The vice list concludes with kleptai (thieves), pleonektai (greedy/covetous), methysoi (drunkards), loidoroi (slanderers), harpages (swindlers). None of these, Paul says, will inherit the kingdom (basileian Theou ou klēronomēsousin). The list is not hierarchical — no single vice is worse than others. All represent modes of living oriented away from God and toward self-assertion.
6.11: kai tauta tines ēte (and some of you were these things) — perfect past: the list of vv. 9-10 described some of the Corinthians' pre-conversion lives. The aorist passive triad that follows is the hinge of the sentence: apelousasthe (you were washed — baptism), hēgiasthēte (you were sanctified — set apart/made holy), edikaiōthēte (you were justified — declared righteous before God). All three are aorist passives: God's action, not the believer's. The agency is double: en tō onomati tou Kyriou Iēsou Christou (in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ) and en tō pneumati tou Theou hēmōn (by the Spirit of our God). Identity transformation through divine action is the ground for the ethical demand.
6.12: panta moi exestin (I have the right to do anything) — twice quoted, twice qualified. This is a Corinthian slogan, not Paul's own assertion. The community apparently argued that Christian freedom (grace, not law) entails no moral constraints. Paul does not deny the slogan but applies two limits: all ou panta sympherei (not everything is beneficial/profitable) — the communal frame; and all ouk egō exousiasthēsomai hypo tinos (I will not be mastered by anything) — the personal frame. The word exousia (authority/right) recurs in different grammatical forms: freedom is not the abolition of authority but its proper exercise.
6.13: ta brōmata tē koilia kai hē koilia tois brōmasin (food for the stomach and the stomach for food) — another Corinthian slogan Paul quotes before demolishing. The argument: just as digestion is morally neutral, so sex is morally neutral. Paul's counter is christological: the body is ou tē porneia alla tō Kyriō (not for sexual immorality but for the Lord), and the Lord is for the body. This moves the body out of the category of morally neutral biological function and into the category of Christ's own — a property relationship that reframes all bodily ethics.
6.14: ho de Theos kai ton Kyrion ēgeiren kai hēmas exegerei (by his power God raised the Lord from the dead, and he will raise us also) — the resurrection of Christ establishes the body's eschatological dignity. If the body participates in resurrection, it cannot be treated as a morally neutral organism. The future exegerei (will raise) is the ground of the present ethics: the body that will be raised is the body that is now joined to Christ.
6.15: ouk oidate hoti ta sōmata hymōn melē Christou estin (do you not know that your bodies are members of Christ?) — melē (members, limbs) is the body-of-Christ vocabulary: the individual body is a member of Christ's own body. aras oun ta melē tou Christou poiēsō pornēs melē (shall I then take the members of Christ and make them members of a prostitute?) — the question is rhetorical and its answer (mē genoito, never!) is Paul's strongest negative formula, used only when the option is theologically impossible.
6.16: kollōmenos tē pornē hen sōma estin (the one who unites himself with a prostitute is one body with her) — Paul applies the one-flesh principle from Gen 2:24 (esontai hoi dyo eis sarka mian) to the prostitute encounter. The sexual union creates a real bodily bond (hen sōma), not a merely physical exchange. The argument depends on the body's non-neutrality: sex does something metaphysically, not just biologically.
6.17: ho de kollōmenos tō Kyriō hen pneuma estin (but the one who is united to the Lord is one spirit with him) — the parallelism with v. 16 is deliberate. Both relationships are characterized by union (kollaō, cleave/join); both produce a oneness; but the union with the Lord is pneumatic (spiritual), not merely somatic. This does not spiritualize the body away — the body is the site of both unions — but establishes the qualitative difference between the two allegiances.
6.18: pheugete tēn porneian (flee from sexual immorality) — aorist imperative of flight, the same word used of Joseph fleeing Potiphar's wife (LXX Gen 39:12). The call to flee rather than resist is significant: porneia is not an enemy to engage but a snare to escape. pan hamartēma ho ean poiēsē anthrōpos ektos tou sōmatos estin — every sin a person commits is outside the body; but sexual immorality is eis to idion sōma (against one's own body). The precise meaning of this uniqueness is debated, but Paul's point is that bodily sexual sin constitutes a distinctive violation of the body's integrity as the locus of Christ-union and Spirit-indwelling.
6.20: ēgorasthēte gar timēs (you were bought at a price) — the commercial-legal metaphor: the believer has been purchased from their prior owner (sin, death, the powers). Agorazō is the marketplace verb for buying; used of slave redemption. The implication is a transfer of ownership: they are not their own (ouk este heautōn). doxasate dē ton Theon en tō sōmati hymōn (therefore glorify God with your body) — the imperativis grounded in the indicative: because you were purchased, honor God with the body that belongs to him. The body is the site of glorification, not an obstacle to it.

8.6: heis theos ho pater ex hou ta panta kai hemeis eis auton kai heis kyrios Iesous Christos di hou ta panta kai hemeis di autou: Paul's two-clause Christological declaration is a deliberate restructuring of the Shema (Deut 6:4: YHWH our God, YHWH is one). Paul does not replace the Shema but expands it: the 'one God' (Father) and the 'one Lord' (Jesus Christ) together constitute the Shema's unity. The prepositions mark distinct roles: ex hou (from whom) for the Father as source; di hou (through whom) for the Son as agent/mediator. This is one of Paul's most explicit pre-Nicene Trinitarian formulas, set within a polemical argument about idols — the God who is one now includes Jesus Christ.
8.1: peri de tōn eidōlothytōn (now about food offered to idols) — the fifth Corinthian topic introduced by peri de. Eidōlothyton (idol-meat): food sacrificed to a deity in a temple context, then available for purchase in the market or served at civic/social banquets. The issue was pervasive in Greco-Roman urban life; most social occasions involved food in some way connected to temple sacrifice. gnōsis physioī, hē de agapē oikodomeī (knowledge puffs up, but love builds up) — physioō (puff up, inflate): Paul reuses the Corinthian-disease vocabulary from 4:6; 4:18; 5:2. Agapē oikodomeī (love builds up): oikodomeō is Paul's primary communal-building metaphor (1 Cor 3:9-15; 14:3-5). Translation note flags agapē as distinct from philia and erōs.
8.2: ei tis dokei egnōkenai ti, oupō egnō kathōs dei gnōnai (anyone who thinks they know something does not yet know as they ought to know) — the two gnō forms contrast: egnōkenai (to have known: the arrogant self-assessment) and egnō kathōs dei gnōnai (to know as one ought to know: the standard not yet reached). Paul does not deny that knowledge is possible but reframes what it means to truly know: genuine knowledge is always accompanied by the awareness of one's remaining ignorance.
8.3: ei de tis agapā ton Theon, houtos egnōstai hyp' autou (but if anyone loves God, this person is known by God) — the shift from knowing to being known: the passive egnōstai (has been known) makes the relationship asymmetric in an important direction. It is not that the one who loves God thereby knows God better (though they may); it is that they are known by God — which is the ground of the relationship. This echoes Paul's argument in Gal 4:9: you have come to know God, or rather to be known by God.
8.4: ouden eidōlon en kosmō (an idol has no real existence in the world) — ouden (nothing, no reality): the idol is nothing because there is no corresponding divine reality behind it. oudeis Theos ei mē heis (there is no God but one) — the Shema-form of Jewish monotheism (Deut 6:4) adapted: Jewish piety expressed precisely this conviction about other gods' non-existence. The Corinthian "strong" who have this gnōsis are theologically correct.
8.5: legoumenoi theoi (so-called gods) — Paul's ironic quotation marks: he does not deny the widespread social reality of polytheism (there are indeed many entities worshipped as gods and lords) but refuses them the unqualified title. Eite en ouranō eite epi gēs (whether in heaven or on earth) — the cosmic register of the claim: even the highest celestial powers are merely legomenoi (called, named) gods, not actually divine.
8.7: synētheia tou eidōlou heōs arti (through their long association with idols, literally their habit of the idol until now) — synētheia (custom, long-established association, habit): this is not intellectual confusion but deeply embedded behavioral memory. The person's conscience (syneidēsis) has been formed by years of treating idol-meat as genuinely sacred. Even after conversion and intellectual assent to monotheism, the emotional-behavioral reflex remains. asthenēs ousa molynestai (being weak, is defiled) — the weak conscience is defiled not because the food is objectively defiling but because the person eats it with the consciousness of it as truly offered to a god.
8.8: brōma de hēmas ou parastisei tō Theō (food does not bring us closer to God) — the neutral status of food: neither eating nor abstaining makes one closer to or further from God. This is the "strong" position that Paul actually affirms — the knowledge of the strong is correct. The argument that follows (vv. 9-13) does not refute the gnōsis but asks whether the gnōsis is being exercised in a way that builds or destroys.
8.9: blepete de mē pōs hē exousia hymōn autē proskomma genētai tois asthenousi (but be careful that this right of yours does not become a stumbling block to the weak) — exousia (right, authority, freedom): the word Paul uses throughout 1 Cor 7-10 for legitimate freedom. Proskomma (stumbling block, obstacle): the same word as Rom 14:13 — the exercise of legitimate freedom becomes a moral obstacle for the weaker believer.
8.10: oidomelēthēsetai eis to ta eidōlothyta esthiein (won't they be encouraged/built up to eat idol-meat?) — the irony of the verb: oikodomeō (build up) is Paul's positive term for communal edification (v. 1; 14:3), but here it denotes a destructive building — the weak believer is being built up in the direction of their destruction. Seeing the strong Christian eating in the temple precinct encourages them to participate in a way that violates their own conscience.
8.11: apollytai gar ho asthenōn en tē sē gnōsei, ho adelphos di' hon Christos apethanen (so this weak brother, for whom Christ died, is destroyed by your knowledge) — the Christological weight of the argument: the weak believer is not merely a social inconvenience but one for whom Christ died (di' hon Christos apethanen). The death of Christ for the weak establishes the infinite value of the weak believer's conscience-integrity. Destroying that believer with food is destroying someone whom Christ valued with his life.
8.12: hamartanete eis ton Christon (you are sinning against Christ) — the escalation: sinning against the weak brother's conscience is sinning against Christ himself. Typtontes autōn tēn syneidēsin asthenousan (wounding their weak conscience) — typtō (strike, wound): the violence of the metaphor captures the real damage done to the weak believer's moral integrity when they are pressured by example to violate their own conscience.
8.13: dioper ei brōma skandalizei ton adelphon mou, ou mē phagō krea eis ton aiōna (therefore, if what I eat causes my brother to stumble, I will never eat meat again) — skandalizō (cause to stumble, trip): the foundation of Paul's practical conclusion. The indefinite extension (eis ton aiōna, forever) is rhetorical hyperbole but makes the principle absolute: Paul's freedom over food is entirely subordinate to the welfare of the weak believer. The argument of ch. 8 is completed in ch. 9 (Paul's own example of waiving rights) and ch. 10 (practical guidance on idol-meat situations).

11.24: touto mou estin to soma to hyper hymon klomenon (touto mou estin to sōma to hyper hymōn, 'this is my body which is for you'): Paul's institution narrative (vv. 23-25) is the earliest written account of the Lord's Supper, predating the Gospels. Parelabon ... paredoka (I received ... I delivered) uses the technical Jewish tradition-transmission vocabulary (qibbel... masar): Paul claims this as received tradition, not his own creation. eis ten emen anamnesin (eis tēn emēn anamnēsin, 'in remembrance of me'): anamnēsis in LXX sacrifice contexts (Lev 24:7; Num 10:10) denotes a memorial that makes the past effective in the present — not merely cognitive recall but cultic re-presentation. The Eucharist re-presents Christ's self-offering.
11.1: Be imitators of me as I am of Christ — the imitation chain: Corinthians imitate Paul who imitates Christ. This closing verse of chapter 10 (some manuscripts begin chapter 11 here) anchors the entire self-limitation argument in the pattern of Christ's own self-giving.
11.2: Paul praises them for remembering him in everything and holding to the traditions (paradoseis) as he delivered them — paradosis is the technical term for the handing-on of authoritative teaching; the same word used in 11:23 for the Supper institution.
11.3: The head (kephalē) of every man is Christ, the head of a woman is man, and the head of Christ is God — a relational hierarchy of headship. The meaning of kephalē (source? authority?) is debated; in context it frames the discussion of honorable and dishonorable head-covering practice.
11.4: Every man who prays or prophesies with his head covered dishonors his head — in Roman honor culture, men of social standing wore the toga capite velato (head veiled) at religious ceremonies; Paul appears to be countering a practice that confused Roman civic-religious customs with Christian worship.
11.5: Every woman who prays or prophesies with her head uncovered dishonors her head — women pray and prophesy publicly in the assembly; that is assumed, not questioned. The issue is the head-covering that signals sexual honor in the Mediterranean world.
11.6: If a woman does not cover her head let her also cut off her hair — a reductio ad absurdum; a woman with a shaved head in the ancient Mediterranean signaled shame or mourning; since that is unthinkable, so is going uncovered.
11.7: A man should not cover his head since he is the image and glory of God; but woman is the glory of man — Genesis 1:26-27 is the ground; both image God (Paul does not deny this of women), but the glory-relation Paul describes maps the creation order onto the covering practice.
11.8: Man was not made from woman but woman from man — the creation sequence of Genesis 2 is invoked; woman was taken from man's side (ek tou andros), establishing a derivative relation.
11.9: Man was not created for woman but woman for man — the purpose stated in Genesis 2:18-20: it is not good for man to be alone, so a helper was made for him. Paul reads this as indicating the directional orientation of the original creation.
11.10: A woman ought to have authority (exousia) on her head because of the angels — the exousia is on the woman herself, not over her; most scholars read this as her authority to pray and prophesy, signaled by the covering. The angel reference may allude to Genesis 6 traditions or angelic observers of worship.
11.11: Nevertheless, in the Lord woman is not independent of man nor man of woman — Paul immediately qualifies the asymmetry with mutuality: in the Lord (en kyriō), the relational hierarchy does not mean subordination of worth.
11.12: For as woman came from man, so man is now born through woman, and all things are from God — the interdependence is complete; creation began with woman from man, but every subsequent human comes through woman, and both originate from God.
11.13: Judge for yourselves — is it proper for a woman to pray to God with her head uncovered? — Paul appeals to the Corinthians' own judgment, a rhetorical move that assumes they will agree with him when they think it through.
11.14: Does not nature itself teach you that if a man wears long hair it is a disgrace to him — Paul appeals to physis (nature) meaning conventional expectation; the culturally natural signal of male dignity in the Greek world involved shorter hair.
11.15: But if a woman wears long hair it is her glory (doxa), because her hair was given to her as a covering (peribolaiou) — the long hair itself functions as a natural head-covering, suggesting that the artificial covering mirrors what nature already provides.
11.16: If anyone is inclined to be contentious about this, we have no such practice, nor do the churches of God — Paul closes not with a theological argument but an appeal to universal church practice; the Corinthians are out of step with every other assembly.
11.17: Paul issues a sharper rebuke: in giving these instructions he does not praise them, for they come together not for the better but for the worse — the word is kreisson/hēsson (better/worse); the assembly is actively harmful, not merely neutral.
11.18: He hears there are divisions (schismata) among them when they meet — the schismata of 1:10-12 (party factions) have now infected the eucharistic assembly itself, the one place that should most express unity.
11.19: There must also be factions (haireseis) among you so that those who are genuine (dokimoi) may be recognized among you — a grim concession: divisions reveal who truly belongs. The word hairesis (faction, later heresy) will carry heavy freight in church history.
11.20: When you come together it is not the Lord's Supper that you eat (kyriakon deipnon) — the word kyriakon (belonging to the Lord) is used only here and in Revelation 1:10 (the Lord's Day) in the NT; a technical early designation for the community meal.
11.21: Each one goes ahead with his own meal — they are not sharing but eating their own provisions; the wealthy eat and drink well while the poor go hungry. The economic inequality of Corinthian society has invaded the sacred meal.
11.22: Do you not have houses to eat and drink in? Or do you despise the church of God and humiliate those who have nothing? — the question is devastating: if you want to eat your own food, do it at home; the assembly is not a private dinner party.
11.23: Paul received from the Lord what he also delivered to you — the language of tradition: paralambanō (receive) and paradidōmi (deliver/hand on) are the technical rabbinic terms for transmitting authoritative teaching. Paul received this from the Lord, not from a human chain.
11.25: The new covenant in my blood — hē kainē diathēkē en tō emō haimati; kainē diathēkē echoes Jeremiah 31:31-34, the new covenant. The Lord's Supper is the enacted ratification of the covenant Jeremiah announced.
11.26: For as often as you eat this bread and drink the cup, you proclaim the Lord's death until he comes — katangellete (proclaim); the Supper is itself a proclamation, an acted-out word. Until he comes (achri hou elthē) gives the practice an eschatological horizon: the meal announces a present absence and a coming presence.
11.27: Whoever eats the bread or drinks the cup of the Lord in an unworthy manner (anaxiōs) will be guilty (enochos) of the body and blood of the Lord — anaxiōs is an adverb of manner, not a statement of personal worthiness; the unworthiness refers to the manner of eating (the divisions and humiliations of vv.18-22).
11.28: Let a person examine himself (dokimazō) and so eat of the bread and drink of the cup — the self-examination (dokimazō, to test for authenticity, the same root as dokimos in v.19) is not about personal sinfulness in general but about discerning the body properly.
11.29: Anyone who eats and drinks without discerning the body (mē diakrinōn to sōma) eats and drinks judgment (krima) on himself — the body to be discerned is probably the community as body of Christ (the social reading) as much as the eucharistic elements; failure to see the poor as members of Christ's body is the precise sin Paul has been addressing.
11.30: That is why many among you are weak and ill and a number have died (koimōntai, fallen asleep) — Paul sees the physical ailments in the Corinthian community as divine discipline connected to the eucharistic abuse. He is not explaining all illness but a specific pattern of judgment.
11.31: But if we judged ourselves truly (diekrinomen), we would not be judged — the verb diakrinō (discern, judge) links back to v.29; proper self-examination anticipates and forestalls divine judgment.
11.32: When we are judged by the Lord we are being disciplined (paideuometha) so that we may not be condemned along with the world — the illness and death Paul mentioned are corrective discipline (paideia), not final condemnation; God chastens believers rather than condemning them.
11.33: So then, brothers, when you come together to eat, wait for one another (ekdechesthe) — the practical resolution: the failure was not waiting for the poor who arrived later; communal waiting repairs the rupture.
11.34: If anyone is hungry let him eat at home so that when you come together it will not be for judgment — the solution is simple: eat your private meals privately; the assembly is for the shared sacred meal, not private consumption. The rest Paul will arrange when he comes.

13.1: ean tais glossais ton anthropon lalo kai ton angelon agapen de me echo: Paul begins the agape-chapter by making love the necessary condition for the validity of every spiritual gift. The 'tongues of angels' (angelon glōssai) was a Second Temple Jewish concept (cf. T. Job 48-50, where Job's daughters speak in angelic tongues) — Paul assumes this as a real category of speech. Without love (agapē), it is chalkos echon (a resounding gong) — the Corinthian competition over gifts produces sound without substance.
13.13: nuni de menei pistis elpis agape ta tria tauta: 'but now these three remain: faith, hope, love.' Nuni de (but now) is Paul's characteristic eschatological-temporal marker: in the present age, before the complete (to teleion) arrives. The three are the characteristic present-age virtues (cf. 1 Thess 1:3; Rom 5:1-5; Col 1:4-5). Meizōn de toutōn he agapē (the greatest of these is love): not greater because it outlasts faith and hope (though it does — in the age to come there will be sight, not faith; possession, not hope), but greater because love is the nature of God himself (1 John 4:8) and therefore the eschatological reality into which faith and hope resolve.
13.2: ean echō prophēteian kai eidō ta mystēria panta (if I have prophecy and understand all mysteries): the second hypothetical escalation — not just tongues but the highest intellectual spiritual gifts. Mystēria = hidden divine purposes now revealed (cf. 2:7). Ean echō pasan tēn pistin hōste orē methistanai (if I have all faith to move mountains): faith here as a specific miraculous gift (cf. 12:9), not saving faith; methistanai orē echoes Jesus's saying (Matt 17:20). Outheis eimi (I am nothing) — the total negation: without love, the spiritual superstar is ontologically empty.
13.3: ean psōmisō panta ta hyparchonta mou (if I give away all my possessions in pieces): psōmizō = to feed by morsels, distribute piece by piece — not simply 'give all' but a deliberate act of incremental charitable distribution. Ean paradō to sōma mou hina kauchēsomai — the textual variant is significant: kauchēsomai (that I may boast, better attested) vs. kauthesomai (that I may be burned, martyrdom reference). If kauchēsomai is correct, even self-impoverishment done for pride rather than love profits nothing. The criterion is not the act but the motive.
13.4: hē agapē makrothymei, chrēsteuetai (love is patient, love is kind): makrothymia = long-temper, long patience under provocation (the compound of makros = long + thymos = passion/anger). Chrēsteuetai is a NT hapax — perhaps Paul's coinage from chrēstos (useful, kind); it captures active kindness in service. Ou phūsioutai (is not puffed up): physioō = to inflate — the sixth and final use of this term in 1 Corinthians, each targeting Corinthian pride (4:6, 18-19; 5:2; 8:1; 13:4).
13.5: ouk aschēmonei (does not act shamefully/dishonorably): aschēmoneō from aschēmōn = unshapely, dishonorable — love does not behave in ways that violate social propriety and honor. Ou zētei ta heautēs (does not seek its own interests): the anti-self-seeking formula that opposes the Corinthian competition for status. Ou paroxizetai (is not provoked/irritated): paroxizō = to sharpen, to irritate — love does not have a hair trigger for anger. Ou logizestai to kakon (does not count the evil done to it) — logizestai = to calculate, keep accounts; love does not keep a ledger of wrongs.
13.6: ou chairei epi tē adikia (does not rejoice at wrongdoing): adikia = injustice, unrighteousness — the same term used for the lawsuit-behavior of 6:8. Love does not find pleasure in another's moral failure (whether in the sense of Schadenfreude or of using someone's sin as an opportunity). Sygchairei de tē alētheia (but rejoices with the truth): the syn-compound (sygchairō = to rejoice together with) — love rejoices not merely at truth but in company with it, aligning its emotions with the way things actually are.
13.7: panta stegei... panta elpizei... panta hypomenei (love endures all things... hopes all things... perseveres all things): stegō = to cover, to endure by covering over (the root means roof-tile — what keeps out rain). The fourfold panta (all things) is emphatic and absolute. Hypomenō = to remain under a load, to persevere without moving from under pressure. The two pairs (believes/hopes and endures/perseveres) are both cognitive-dispositional and volitional-endurance aspects of love's totality.
13.8: hē agapē oudepote piptei (love never falls/fails): piptō = to fall — love never collapses under any pressure or into obsolescence. Ai prophēteiai katargēthēsontai (prophecies will be abolished): katargeō = to render inoperative, to nullify — the verb used throughout Paul for the abolition of obsolete covenantal arrangements (the Law, death). The charismatic gifts are functionally temporary; love is eschatologically permanent because God is love (1 John 4:8) and the age to come is God's own life.
13.9: ek merous gar ginōskontes kai ek merous prophēteuontes (for we know in part and we prophesy in part): ek merous = out of the partial, from a portion — the present epistemic and prophetic condition is inherently partial, bounded by the limits of the present age. Both knowledge (gnōsis) and prophecy (prophēteia) — the two most intellectually prestigious gifts in Corinth — are equally limited in the current dispensation. No gift gives full access to divine reality in this age.
13.10: hotan de elthē to teleion (when the complete/perfect comes): teleios = complete, mature, fully accomplished — not 'perfect' in the modern sense of flawlessness but complete in the sense of full realization. The debate over what to teleion refers to (the NT canon? personal maturity? the eschaton/parousia?) is exegetically significant. The eschatological reading fits best: when the whole arrives, the part is obsoleted — the same logic as the childhood/adulthood analogy of v. 11 and the mirror/face-to-face contrast of v. 12.
13.11: hote ēmēn nēpios elaloun hōs nēpios (when I was a child I spoke as a child): nēpios = infant/young child — Paul uses this term elsewhere for spiritual immaturity (3:1: 'I could not speak to you as spiritual people but as fleshly people, as infants in Christ'). Katērgēka ta tou nēpiou (I gave up childish things) — the same katargeō verb as v. 8: the abolition of the partial when the complete arrives. The childhood-to-adulthood transition is the analogy for the current-age-to-coming-age transition.
13.12: blepomen gar arti di' esoptrou en ainigmati (for now we see through a mirror in a riddle/enigma): esoptron = polished metal mirror (ancient mirrors gave dim, indirect reflections); ainigma = riddle, dark/indirect saying — the LXX uses it for Num 12:8 where Moses sees God 'face to face' in contrast to the prophets who see only en ainigmati. Present spiritual sight is indirect and partial; the eschatological vision is direct: prosōpon pros prosōpon (face to face). Epignōsomai kathōs kai epegnōsthēn (I will know fully, even as I have been fully known) — the passive divine knowing precedes and will perfect the human knowing.

15.3: paredoka gar hymin en protois ho kai parelabon (paredōka gar hymin en prōtois ho kai parelabon): the creedal formula of vv. 3-5 is the earliest extended theological statement in the NT, predating Paul's letters by roughly a decade after the crucifixion (30-35 CE). The four elements follow a strict parallelism: Christ died / was buried / was raised / appeared. kata tas graphas (twice): both the death and the resurrection are 'according to the Scriptures' — Scripture is the interpretive framework that gives the events their meaning; the cross is not a tragic accident but a divinely-ordained fulfillment of the entire OT witness.
15.20: nuni de Christos egetertai ek nekron aparche ton kekoimemenon (nuni de Christos egēgertai ek nekrōn aparchē tōn kekoimēmenōn): Aparche (firstfruits) is the festival term for the first portion offered to God that consecrates and guarantees the whole (Lev 23:10-11; Num 18:12). By calling Christ the firstfruits of those who sleep, Paul makes his resurrection the beginning of the general resurrection, not an isolated miraculous event. The resurrection of believers is therefore not a secondary hope but structurally necessary: if Christ is firstfruits, the harvest will follow. Kekoimēmenōn (those who sleep) is the NT euphemism for death that Paul's resurrection-theology recontextualizes: sleep implies waking.
15.55: pou sou thanate to nikos pou sou thanate to kentron (pou sou, thanate, to nikos; pou sou, thanate, to kentron): Paul adapts Hos 13:14 and Isa 25:8 into a victory-taunt over death. Kentron (sting/goad) was used for the venomous sting of a scorpion or the goading hook of a livestock driver — death's power to wound and compel. Paul names sin as death's sting (v. 56: to de kentron tou thanatou he hamartia) — death derives its terror from the law's capacity to count sin against us. Christ's death absorbs both the sting (sin's debt) and the goad (law's condemnation), leaving death powerless. The taunt is eschatologically realized, not merely future.
15.1: Paul makes known (gnōrizō) the gospel (euangelion) he preached — the present tense indicates an ongoing announcement, not a completed past event. He frames chapter 15 as a reminder of what they already received and on which they stand.
15.2: Through the gospel they are being saved (sōzesthe, present passive) — salvation is a process in motion, not merely a past event. The conditional unless you believed in vain (eikē) introduces the stakes: misbelief about resurrection nullifies the whole.
15.4: That he was buried and that he was raised on the third day in accordance with the scriptures (kata tas graphas) — the burial confirms the reality of death; the resurrection is scripture-fulfillment, though Paul does not name which texts. The passive ēgertai (was raised) emphasizes divine action throughout.
15.5: He appeared (ōphthē, aorist passive, he was seen) to Cephas, then to the Twelve — the verb ōphthē is the standard LXX verb for divine appearances (theophanies); Paul uses it consistently for all the resurrection appearances listed here.
15.6: He appeared to more than five hundred brothers at one time, most of whom are still alive, though some have fallen asleep — Paul signals that witnesses can be interviewed; this is not pious tradition but verifiable testimony.
15.7: He appeared to James, then to all the apostles — James the Lord's brother, who was not among the Twelve but became a pillar of the Jerusalem church (Gal 1:19; 2:9); his conversion is traceable to this appearance.
15.8: Last of all, as to one untimely born (ektrōmati, a miscarriage or aborted fetus), he appeared also to Paul — the word ektroma conveys abnormality and premature or violent birth; Paul uses it of his own out-of-sequence call, acknowledging that he was not a disciple during Jesus's ministry.
15.9: Paul is the least of the apostles, not worthy to be called an apostle, because he persecuted the church of God — the verb ediōxa (I persecuted) is imperfect, ongoing past action; Paul never lets himself forget the violence of his pre-conversion life.
15.10: By the grace of God I am what I am — one of Paul's most direct statements of identity. The charis (grace) of God was not in vain (kene); Paul labored more abundantly than all the apostles, yet insists it was not himself but the grace of God working with him.
15.11: Whether then it was I or they, so we preach and so you believed — the kerygma is identical across all the apostolic witnesses; the content, not the messenger, is the fixed point.
15.12: Now if Christ is proclaimed as raised from the dead, how can some among you say there is no resurrection of the dead? — the rhetorical question identifies the logical contradiction: the Corinthians affirm Christ's resurrection while denying the general resurrection, though the two stand or fall together.
15.13: If there is no resurrection of the dead, then Christ has not been raised — the argument moves by strict logical inference; the denial of the category (resurrection of the dead) eliminates the particular instance (Christ's resurrection).
15.14: And if Christ has not been raised, then our proclamation is empty (kenon) and your faith is empty (kenē) — both kenon (preaching) and kenē (faith) echo the word Paul used in v.10 (the grace that was not in vain/kene); if the resurrection is false, everything becomes kenon.
15.15: We are found to be false witnesses about God because we testified about God that he raised Christ — the word pseudomartyres (false witnesses) is the same category as the Ninth Commandment violation; to preach a resurrection that did not happen makes the apostles liars about God.
15.16: For if the dead are not raised, not even Christ has been raised — the argument repeats for emphasis; the logical entailment must be stated twice to make clear that there is no escape hatch.
15.17: And if Christ has not been raised, your faith is futile (mataia) and you are still in your sins — mataia (empty, futile) is a stronger word than kene; it means utterly without result. Without resurrection, the atonement is incomplete.
15.18: Then also those who have fallen asleep in Christ have perished (apōlonto) — the aorist of apollymi (to destroy, to perish); this is not a gentle word. If there is no resurrection, the Christian dead are simply gone.
15.19: If we have hoped in Christ only for this life, we are of all people most to be pitied (eleenoteroi) — the superlative adjective; apostolic suffering (v.30-32) makes no sense without resurrection. Hope that ends at death makes Christians the most pathetic of all people.
15.21: For as by a man came death, by a man has also come the resurrection of the dead — the Adam/Christ parallelism that Paul develops more fully in Romans 5:12-21 is here introduced through the category of manhood: a human brought death; a human has also brought resurrection.
15.22: For as in Adam all die, so also in Christ shall all be made alive — the two spheres: en tō Adam (in Adam) and en tō Christō (in Christ); membership in these two figures determines one's eschatological destiny. The all in each case is governed by the respective union.
15.23: But each in his own order (tagmati) — tagma is a military term for a battalion or rank; Paul imagines the resurrection as a military procession. Christ the firstfruits (aparche), then at his coming (parousia) those who belong to Christ.
15.24: Then comes the end, when he delivers the kingdom to God the Father after destroying every rule and every authority and power — the sequence: resurrection, then the eschatological defeat of hostile powers, then the handing over of the kingdom. The verb katargein (destroy, render inoperative) will recur in v.26.
15.25: For he must reign until he has put all his enemies under his feet — citing Psalm 110:1, the most-quoted OT text in the NT. The dei (must) indicates divine necessity rooted in the Psalm's promise; Christ's reign is purposively aimed at the defeat of every hostile power.
15.26: The last enemy to be destroyed (katargeitai) is death — death is personified as a power, the final adversary; the verb katargeitai (present passive, is being destroyed, will be destroyed) is the same word used for all the hostile powers in v.24.
15.27: For God has put all things in subjection under his feet — citing Psalm 8:6; the human figure of Psalm 8 (what is man?) receives dominion over all creation. Paul reads this as eschatologically fulfilled in Christ. But when it says all things are put in subjection, it is plain that he is excepted who put all things in subjection under him.
15.28: When all things are subjected to him, then the Son himself will also be subjected to him who put all things in subjection under him, so that God may be all in all — the telos (end) of the entire redemptive sequence: the Son's subjection to the Father is not subordination of being but the completion of the mission; God becomes ta panta en pasin (all things in all).
15.29: Otherwise what do people mean by being baptized for (or on behalf of) the dead? — baptism hyper tōn nekrōn is a notorious crux; Paul does not endorse or explain the practice but uses it as an argument ad hominem: if there is no resurrection, why are some of you doing this?
15.30: Why are we in danger every hour? — Paul's first-person argument from experience; his constant peril makes no sense if there is no resurrection.
15.31: I die every day — Paul's self-description of daily apostolic risk; the hyperbolic claim that he dies daily is grounded in the constant threat of martyrdom.
15.32: If from human motives I fought with beasts at Ephesus, what do I gain? If the dead are not raised, let us eat and drink for tomorrow we die — citing Isaiah 22:13; the Epicurean pleasure-principle becomes the only rational response if resurrection is false. Paul may refer to a real or metaphorical beast-fight at Ephesus.
15.33: Do not be deceived: bad company ruins good morals — Paul cites a well-known line from Menander's comedy Thais (phtheirousin ethe chresta homiliai kakai); the Corinthians' association with those who deny resurrection is corrupting their character.
15.34: Wake up from your drunken stupor (eknepsate) as you ought and do not go on sinning — the verb eknephō (sober up, become sane again) implies intoxication of thought; some at Corinth have no knowledge (agnosian) of God, which Paul says to their shame.
15.35: But someone will ask, how are the dead raised? With what kind of body do they come? — Paul shifts to the second question, which he treats as foolish (aphrōn): not the fact of resurrection but its mode.
15.36: You foolish person (aphrōn) — what you sow does not come to life unless it dies — the agricultural analogy: the seed must die before it can become a plant. The continuity between seed and plant is real but the form is transformed.
15.37: And what you sow is not the body that is to be, but a bare kernel, perhaps of wheat or some other grain — the seed is the material continuity; the body that emerges is the transformed form. Paul does not specify the relation precisely but insists there is both continuity and transformation.
15.38: But God gives it a body as he has chosen, and to each kind of seed its own body — the diversity of bodies is God's design; there is no single form of embodied existence.
15.39: Not all flesh is the same flesh — sarx (flesh): human, animal, bird, fish each have different sarx. The word sarx does not mean body in general; it specifies material constitution.
15.40: There are heavenly bodies and earthly bodies, but the glory of the heavenly is of one kind and the glory of the earthly is of another — sōmata epourania (heavenly bodies, i.e. stars) and sōmata epigeia (earthly bodies, i.e. living creatures). Each kind of body has its own doxa (glory, radiance).
15.41: There is one glory of the sun, and another glory of the moon, and another glory of the stars; for star differs from star in glory — the differentiation of glory within a single category (stars) supports the idea that the resurrection body will be glorious yet differentiated from the present body.
15.42: So is it with the resurrection of the dead — the analogy is applied. What is sown in perishability (phthora), raised in imperishability (aphtharsia); the four contrasts in vv. 42-44 are all passive participles: it is sown... it is raised...
15.43: Sown in dishonor (atimia), raised in glory (doxa); sown in weakness (astheneia), raised in power (dynamis) — the four pairs map the transformation: mortal weakness and dishonor are transformed into glory and power.
15.44: Sown a natural body (sōma psychikon), raised a spiritual body (sōma pneumatikon) — psychikon does not mean soulless or non-spiritual in a modern sense; it means animated by the psyche (natural life-principle). Pneumatikon means animated by the pneuma (Spirit of God); the resurrection body is fully animated by divine Spirit.
15.45: So it is written: the first man Adam became a living soul (psychen zōsan) — citing Genesis 2:7 (LXX); the last Adam became a life-giving spirit (pneuma zōopoioun). The contrast is between receiving life (psychen zōsan) and giving life (zōopoioun); the risen Christ is the source and agent of resurrection life.
15.46: But it is not the spiritual that is first but the natural and then the spiritual — the sequence matters: the psychikon precedes the pneumatikon in human experience. The order of the ages follows: present age (psychikon), then the age to come (pneumatikon).
15.47: The first man is from the earth, made of dust (choikos); the second man is from heaven — choikos (earthy, made of earth-stuff) echoes Genesis 2:7 (dust/aphar); the second man is from heaven (ex ouranou), indicating Christ's origin and the nature of the resurrection body.
15.48: As was the man of dust, so also are those who are of the dust; and as is the man of heaven, so also are those who are of heaven — the two humanities: earthy humanity descended from Adam, heavenly humanity participating in Christ's risen nature.
15.49: Just as we have borne the image of the man of dust, we shall also bear the image of the man of heaven — eikōn (image) carries the weight of Genesis 1:26-27; those who now bear Adam's image will bear Christ's image. Some manuscripts read the hortatory subjunctive (let us also bear) rather than the future indicative.
15.50: Flesh and blood (sarx kai haima) cannot inherit the kingdom of God, nor does the perishable inherit the imperishable — the rhetorical climax; the present bodily constitution is incapable of inheriting the coming order. Transformation, not mere resuscitation, is required.
15.51: Behold, I tell you a mystery (mystērion) — Paul introduces a previously unrevealed truth. We will not all sleep (die), but we will all be changed (allagesometha) — the mystery is the fate of those alive at the parousia: they will be transformed rather than dying and rising.
