15.52: In a moment, in the twinkling of an eye (en atomō, en rhipē ophthalmou), at the last trumpet — atomos (indivisible moment) and rhipe ophthalmou (flash of an eye) are Greek expressions for the fastest conceivable instant. The last trumpet signals eschatological completion.
15.53: For this perishable body must put on the imperishable, and this mortal body must put on immortality — ependysasthai (put on, clothe oneself with) as one puts on a garment; the imagery is of clothing the mortal with the divine attribute of athanasia (immortality).
15.54: When the perishable puts on the imperishable, and the mortal puts on immortality, then shall come to pass the saying that is written: Death is swallowed up in victory — citing Isaiah 25:8 (LXX: death swallowed up in victory / katepothē ho thanatos eis nikos). The verb katapinō (swallow up, devour) reverses the image of death as a devourer.
15.56: The sting of death is sin, and the power of sin is the law — a parenthetical theological statement that answers why death had such power: sin gives death its sting (kentron, the point of a weapon or sting of an insect), and the law gives sin its power by defining and amplifying transgression (Rom 5:20; 7:7-11).
15.57: But thanks be to God (tō de theō charis) who gives us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ — the doxological eruption that follows the citation of Isaiah 25:8; the victory is gift (charis, grace), not achievement.
15.58: Therefore, my beloved brothers, be steadfast (hedraioi), immovable (ametakinetoi), always abounding in the work of the Lord, knowing that in the Lord your labor is not in vain (kenon) — kenon (empty/vain) from v.14 returns: the labor that would have been empty if resurrection were false is now full of meaning because the resurrection is true.

4.1: ὑπηρέτας Χριστοῦ (servants of Christ) — hypēretēs was originally the under-rower on a trireme (the lowest rank of rowers below the main deck), then generalized to mean subordinate assistant. It conveys harder, more subordinate labor than diakonos. Οἰκονόμους μυστηρίων θεοῦ (stewards of the mysteries of God) — the oikonomos was a slave-manager trusted with the master's household and finances; responsible, trusted, but accountable to the master alone, not to the household.
4.2: ζητεῖται ἐν τοῖς οἰκονόμοις ἵνα πιστός τις εὑρεθῇ (what is sought in stewards is that a faithful person be found) — present passive zēteitai: the ongoing requirement. Pistos (faithful/trustworthy) returns to the root of pistis; the steward's virtue is not eloquence or wisdom but reliability in the master's service. The passive heurethē (be found [faithful]) anticipates the eschatological evaluation of 4:5.
4.3: εἰς ἐλάχιστόν ἐστιν (it is a very little thing to me) — the superlative of mikros: it is the very smallest matter imaginable. Paul relativizes the Corinthian judgment to near-zero. Ἀνθρωπίνης ἡμέρας (human day) — a wordplay on hēmera kyriou (the Day of the Lord): the Corinthians are holding a human day (a human tribunal), not the divine Day.
4.4: οὐδὲν γὰρ ἐμαυτῷ σύνοιδα (for I am not conscious of anything against myself) — synoida (to be a co-witness with oneself, to be aware of as a shared knowledge) is a self-knowledge term: Paul's own conscience is clear. But the double negation qualifies this immediately: all ouk en toutō dedikaiōmai (but I have not been justified by this). Clean conscience ≠ divine acquittal; the competent judge is not Paul himself.
4.5: κρίνετε μηδὲν πρὸ καιροῦ (judge nothing before the time) — present imperative: stop the ongoing practice of judging. Heōs an elthē ho kyrios (until the Lord comes) — aorist subjunctive in a temporal clause; the judgment belongs to the Parousia. The Lord will phōtizei (illuminate) what is hidden and phanerōsei (manifest, make visible) the purposes of hearts — two future indicatives for the eschatological disclosure that renders present judgments premature.
4.6: μετεσχημάτισα εἰς ἐμαυτὸν καὶ Ἀπολλῶν (I have transferred [these things] in figure to myself and Apollos) — metaschēmatizō (to transform the form/figure of) indicates that Paul has been using himself and Apollos as representative figures for a broader principle. The actual targets of the criticism are other leaders or factions in Corinth. Τὸ μὴ ὑπὲρ ἃ γέγραπται (the not beyond what stands written) — the cryptic phrase may be an early proverbial rule about scriptural limits; possibly: do not go beyond what scripture has set down in your evaluations of teachers.
4.7: τί δὲ ἔχεις ὃ οὐκ ἔλαβες (but what do you have that you did not receive?) — the rhetorical question delivers the demolition: all gifts are received, not achieved. Ει δε και ελαβες, τί καυχᾶσαι ως μὴ λαβών (and if you did receive, why do you boast as though you had not received?) — the aorist elabes and present kauchāsai create an irony: the reception was completed; the boasting continues as if it were not.
4.8: ἤδη κεκορεσμένοι ἐστέ (already you are satiated/stuffed) — the perfect passive kekoresmenoi: they have achieved a state of satiation and remain in it. The irony is thick: the Corinthians act as if they have already arrived at eschatological fullness. Ἐβασιλεύσατε (you began to reign) — aorist: they inaugurated their reign. Chōris hēmōn (without us): without the apostles who are still suffering. Paul's apostolic hardship catalogue (vv.9-13) will make the contrast devastating.
4.9: ἐπιθανατίους (epithanatious = condemned to die) — a hapax legomenon in Paul (only here in NT): those condemned to death who were exhibited last in the gladiatorial procession for maximum dramatic effect. The spectacle (theatron) is the same word as theater: Paul and the apostles are exhibited as a theatrical spectacle to the cosmos, angels, and humans. The triadic audience (cosmos, angels, humans) is cosmic in scope.
4.10: ἡμεῖς μωροὶ διὰ Χριστόν (we are fools because of Christ) — the emphatic pronoun hēmeis (we apostles) contrasts with the implied hymeis (you Corinthians) in each antithesis: we are fools — you are wise; we are weak — you are strong; we are without honor — you are honored. Each antithesis is sarcastic: the Corinthians have claimed what the apostles have not yet received, demonstrating their realized-eschatology confusion.
4.11: ἄχρι τῆς ἄρτι ὥρας (until this very hour) — the precision is temporal: not in the past, not hypothetically, but to this present moment. The apostolic hardship list (hungry, thirsty, in rags, brutally treated, homeless) is present reality, not rhetorical hyperbole. The list serves as evidence against the Corinthian triumphalism: the apostolic paradigm is current, not historical.
4.12: κοπιῶμεν ἐργαζόμενοι ταῖς ἰδίαις χερσίν (we labor working with our own hands) — present participle: ongoing manual labor, which in the Greek social hierarchy was the activity of the lower classes and artisans, not of the educated. Paul deliberately places himself in the social class below the Corinthians who valorize wisdom and status. The behavioral responses (eulogoumen... anechometha... parakaloumen — we bless... we endure... we gently answer) move from verb to verb with asyndeton, each a present tense response pattern.
4.13: περικαθάρματα τοῦ κόσμου ἐγενήθημεν (we became the scum of the world) — aorist passive: the state they were brought into. Perikatharmata (scrapings around the outside, filth cleaned off) was used for the waste matter scraped from a pot and also for persons devoted as scapegoat-sacrifices to cleanse a city from pollution. Peripsēma (refuse, off-scrapings) reinforces: the apostolic ministry places them at the absolute bottom of social status.
4.14: ἐντρέπων γράφω ταῦτα (I am not writing these things to shame you) — present active: the ongoing purpose of the letter is parental correction, not public humiliation. The distinction between entrepō (shame as goal) and noutheteō (admonish, correct) is between punitive and corrective intent. Paul positions himself as father, not adversary.
4.15: ἐν γὰρ Χριστῷ Ἰησοῦ διὰ τοῦ εὐαγγελίου ἐγὼ ὑμᾶς ἐγέννησα (for in Christ Jesus through the gospel I became your father) — aorist: a completed act. The prepositional chain locates the fatherhood in Christ and through the specific instrument of the gospel. Paul's unique claim as father is based on his role as the one who first proclaimed the gospel that brought the community into being — not on ongoing pastoral relationship, which anyone can provide.
4.16: μιμηταί μου γίνεσθε (become imitators of me) — present imperative: begin and keep on becoming. Mimētēs (imitator) carries the sense of modeling and reproducing a pattern, not blind copying. The imitation is not of Paul's personality but of his pattern of cruciform living (the paradigm of vv.9-13). This call to imitation will be grounded in 11:1 with kathōs kagō Christou (as I am of Christ).
4.17: ἀνέμνησεν ὑμᾶς (will remind you) — future active: Timothy's mission is to bring the Corinthians back to what they already know. The object: tas hodous mou tas en Christō (my ways in Christ) — not merely Paul's apostolic teaching but his way of living, his pattern of conduct. Kathōs pantachou en pasē ekklēsia didaskō (as I teach everywhere in every church): the appeal to universally consistent teaching delegitimizes Corinthian exceptionalism.
4.18: ἐφυσιώθησάν τινες (some have become puffed up) — aorist passive: they were inflated into a state of pride. Phusioō (to inflate, to puff up — from physa, bellows) will appear six more times in 1 Corinthians (4:19; 5:2; 8:1; 13:4; twice in 4:18-19): the letter's running diagnosis of what is wrong in Corinth is arrogant inflation, the opposite of the cruciform deflation Paul models.
4.19: γνώσομαι οὐ τὸν λόγον τῶν πεφυσιωμένων ἀλλὰ τὴν δύναμιν (I will know not the speech of those who are puffed up but the power) — future indicative: a personal declaration of Paul's evaluative criterion when he arrives. The contrast logos (speech, rhetoric) versus dynamis (power) recapitulates the entire argument of chapters 1-4: rhetorical eloquence is not the currency of divine action.
4.20: οὐ γὰρ ἐν λόγῳ ἡ βασιλεία τοῦ θεοῦ ἀλλ᾽ ἐν δυνάμει (for the kingdom of God is not in speech but in power) — the present tense is definitive. Basileia tou theou (kingdom of God) appears rarely in 1 Corinthians (4:20; 6:9-10; 15:24, 50); its presence here concludes the wisdom-cross-power argument with the eschatological reference: the age-to-come order operates on different criteria than this age.
4.21: ἐν ῥάβδῳ ἔλθω πρὸς ὑμᾶς ἢ ἐν ἀγάπῃ πνεύματί τε πραΰτητος (shall I come with a rod or in love and a spirit of gentleness?) — the rhabdos (rod, staff) is the instrument of parental correction in Prov 13:24. The triad agapē (love), pneuma (spirit), praütēs (gentleness) characterizes the preferred mode of the apostolic visit. The choice Paul offers is not between wrath and indifference but between two modes of fatherly presence — correctional or gentle — depending on the community's response before he arrives.

9.1: Paul opens with four rapid rhetorical questions — am I not free? am I not an apostle? have I not seen Jesus our Lord? are you not my work in the Lord? — each expects a yes. The Greek eleutheros (free) and apostolos are his twin credentials: freedom from human authority and commission from Christ himself.
9.2: Even if others deny his apostleship, the Corinthians cannot — they are the seal (sphragis) of his apostolate in the Lord, a term from official documents that validated legal claims.
9.3: This is Paul's defense (apologia, the legal term for a formal reply to accusers) to those who examine (anakrinousi) him — judicial language signaling that a formal charge against his apostleship is in view.
9.4: Do we not have the right (exousia) to eat and drink? — exousia recurs seven times in this chapter; Paul's argument is precisely about legitimate authority and the choice to waive it.
9.5: Do we not have the right to take along a believing wife (adelphen gynaika, literally a sister-woman, i.e. a Christian wife)? — Paul mentions Cephas (Peter) and the brothers of the Lord as examples who exercised this right, grounding his argument in recognized apostolic practice.
9.6: Or is it only Barnabas and I who have no right to refrain from working for a living? — Paul and Barnabas are unique in funding their own ministry; the rhetorical force is that their self-support is the anomaly, not the norm.
9.7: Three analogies from common life: a soldier who serves at his own expense, a farmer who plants but never eats the fruit, a shepherd who keeps a flock but gets no milk — each is absurd, making the case that workers deserve their wages.
9.8: Paul is not relying on human reasoning alone — he asks whether the Law itself does not say the same thing, raising the argument to the level of scripture.
9.9: The Mosaic text cited: do not muzzle an ox while it treads out the grain (Deut 25:4) — Paul asks pointedly whether God's concern is really for oxen, signaling that the text carries a deeper human application.
9.10: Paul asserts the text was written for our sake — the plowman plows in hope, the thresher threshes in hope of sharing the crop. The agricultural imagery transfers directly to apostolic labor and its rightful reward.
9.11: If we sowed spiritual things among you, is it too much if we reap material things from you? — the rhetorical question makes the exchange look obviously proportionate: spiritual seed deserves material harvest.
9.12: Others share this right over you — other teachers at Corinth apparently did receive support. Paul and his companions did not exercise this right, but endure all things rather than put any obstacle in the way of the gospel. The word enkopē (obstacle) is a military term for cutting a road to impede an army.
9.13: Those who work in the temple share in what is offered; those who serve at the altar share in the altar's portions — the Jerusalem priestly system supports the analogy: those who minister in holy things receive their sustenance from holy things.
9.14: The Lord commanded that those who proclaim the gospel should get their living from the gospel — a reference to Jesus's instruction to his disciples (Matt 10:10; Luke 10:7: the laborer is worthy of his wages).
9.15: Paul has not used any of these rights and is not writing to claim them now — his boast (kauchema) would be emptied if he did. For him to preach free of charge is the essence of his particular calling.
9.16: Preaching the gospel carries no grounds for boasting — anankē (necessity, compulsion) is laid upon him; woe to him if he does not preach. The word anankē was used of divine compulsion, fate, and moral obligation — Paul places his commission in this category of inescapable necessity.
9.17: If Paul preaches willingly he has a reward (misthos); if unwillingly he is still entrusted with a stewardship (oikonomia) — the oikonomos (steward) manages the master's household goods, not his own; Paul's calling is a trust, not a choice.
9.18: His wage is precisely this: to offer the gospel free of charge, not making full use of his right in the gospel — the paradox is complete. His pay is the act of working for free.
9.19: Free from all (eleutheros ek pantōn), Paul made himself a slave to all so that he might win the more — the same root word (eleutheros/slave) that opened chapter 9 now drives the conclusion: freedom is exercised through voluntary servitude.
9.20: To Jews he became as a Jew — not abandoning his own Jewishness (he was already Jewish), but entering the Jewish cultural world fully in order to win those under the Law.
9.21: To those outside the Law (Gentiles) he became as one outside the Law — though he is not outside God's law but within Christ's law (ennomos Christou); the Greek carefully distinguishes being outside Torah from being lawless before God.
9.22: To the weak he became weak — the language of weakness (astheneia) anticipates the strong/weak discussion of chapter 8-10 about idol food; Paul became whatever his hearers needed so that by all means he might save some.
9.23: He does everything for the sake of the gospel so that he might share in its blessings — he is not merely an instrument of the gospel but a fellow-partaker (synkoinonos) in its benefits.
9.24: Do you not know that those who run in a stadium all run but only one receives the prize? — the Greek games (pan-Hellenic athletics) were familiar to Corinthians; Paul shifts to athletic metaphor to press the urgency of self-discipline.
9.25: Everyone who competes (agōnizomenos, from the agōn, the contest) exercises self-control (enkrateuetai) in all things — the agōn word-group carries connotations of life-and-death struggle; enkrateia (self-control) was a key Greek virtue. The athlete does it for a perishable crown (the pine or laurel wreath); Paul runs for an imperishable one.
9.26: Paul does not run aimlessly or box like one beating the air — his ministry is purposeful and aimed; the contrast implies that the Corinthians who think freedom means indulgence are shadow-boxing.
9.27: Paul disciplines his body (hypōpiazō, literally strike under the eye, beat black and blue) and keeps it under control (doulagōgeō, enslave it) lest after preaching to others he himself should be disqualified (adokimos) — the word adokimos (failing the test) is the opposite of dokimos (approved); even an apostle can fail the test of endurance.

10.1: Paul calls the Corinthians brothers — a term of solidarity — and says he does not want them to be ignorant about their fathers, all of whom were under the cloud and passed through the sea. The Exodus generation is Israel's founding narrative, and Paul is about to treat it as a warning for the church.
10.2: All were baptized into Moses in the cloud and in the sea — a striking use of the baptism language the Corinthians knew; the cloud and sea become the waters of their initiation into the Mosaic covenant, as water baptism initiates into Christ.
10.3: All ate the same spiritual food (broma pneumatikon) — the manna of the wilderness, reread as pneumatic, Spirit-bearing food that prefigured the Eucharist.
10.4: All drank the same spiritual drink — the water from the rock, which Paul identifies as Christ who accompanied them. The rock that followed was a midrashic tradition; Paul reads it as a christological presence throughout the wilderness journey.
10.5: Yet with most of them God was not pleased — the Greek uses the word katestrofēsan (they were scattered, overthrown): the same generation that received spiritual gifts perished in the wilderness. Blessing does not guarantee ultimate approval.
10.6: These events became examples (typoi) for us — typos (pattern, type, mold) is the key hermeneutical word; the wilderness events are not mere history but a divinely designed pattern warning against desire for evil things.
10.7: Do not be idolaters as some of them were — citing Exodus 32:6 (the golden calf episode: the people sat down to eat and drink and rose up to play). The link to idol-feast meat in Corinth is direct: eating at idol temples is the church's golden calf moment.
10.8: Do not engage in sexual immorality as some of them did and twenty-three thousand fell in a single day — the reference is to Numbers 25:9 (which says twenty-four thousand; Paul may cite a variant tradition or count differently). Sexual immorality and idolatry were inseparably linked at Corinthian temple festivals.
10.9: Do not put Christ to the test as some of them did and were destroyed by serpents — the serpent episode of Numbers 21:5-6; Paul reads it as testing Christ, affirming Christ's presence in the wilderness (v.4) and applying the warning to the Corinthians' behavior.
10.10: Do not grumble as some of them grumbled and were destroyed by the destroyer — the murmuring narratives of Numbers 11:1 and 14:2; the destroyer (olothreutēs) likely refers to the destroying angel. Grumbling against God's appointed leaders is spiritually lethal.
10.11: These things happened to them as a type (typikōs) and were written for our instruction, upon whom the ends of the ages have come — the church stands at the convergence of the ages; the OT was written with the church in view, serving as Scripture's eschatological function.
10.12: Therefore let anyone who thinks he stands take heed lest he fall — the Corinthians who pride themselves on their gnōsis (knowledge) and freedom are precisely those at greatest risk; spiritual self-confidence is the precondition of the fall.
10.13: No temptation has seized you except what is common to humanity — Paul offers assurance: the Corinthians are not facing superhuman trial. And God is faithful (pistos ho theos) — the same faithfulness that sustained Israel through the wilderness will not allow testing beyond endurance, but will also provide the way of escape (ekbasis).
10.14: Therefore, flee from idolatry (eidōlolatria) — the imperative is strong: pheugete, run away. Not negotiate, not discern case by case, but flee. This is the summary response to the whole argument from Israel's wilderness failures.
10.15: Paul addresses them as sensible (phronimoi) people and invites them to judge what he says — the irony is sharp given their self-flattery about wisdom; now he asks them to exercise real judgment about the Supper.
10.16: The cup of blessing (potērion tēs eulogias) which we bless — the technical language of the Passover fourth cup (the cup of blessing), now used for the Lord's Supper cup. Communion/fellowship (koinōnia) in the blood of Christ — koinōnia means participation, sharing, solidarity. The cup creates real fellowship with Christ.
10.17: Because there is one bread, we who are many are one body, since we all partake of the one bread — the Eucharist does not merely symbolize unity; sharing the one loaf constitutes the one body. The logic is participatory.
10.18: Consider Israel according to the flesh — those who eat the sacrifices are partners (koinōnoi) with the altar; participation in the temple cult creates real solidarity with what is being worshiped.
10.19: What then am I saying — that an idol is something? or that food sacrificed to idols is something? — Paul is not reversing his position from chapter 8 that idols are nothing; he is shifting the question from ontology to participation.
10.20: What pagans sacrifice they offer to demons, not to God — citing Deuteronomy 32:17 (LXX). The idol is nothing, but behind the idol-cult stands demonic reality; participation in idol feasts creates solidarity with those demonic powers.
10.21: You cannot drink the cup of the Lord and the cup of demons — the two tables are mutually exclusive; koinōnia with Christ and koinōnia with demons are incompatible loyalties. To sit at both is to provoke the Lord.
10.22: Are we provoking the Lord to jealousy (parazēloumen)? — citing Deuteronomy 32:21 from the Song of Moses; the jealousy of God is the response to Israel's idolatry. Are we stronger than he? — the rhetorical question presupposes the answer no.
10.23: All things are permissible — Paul quotes the Corinthian slogan again (as in 6:12), but not all things are beneficial (sympherei) or build up (oikodomei). The criterion shifts from personal rights to communal edification.
10.24: Let no one seek his own good but the good of his neighbor — the ethical principle that governs all the preceding discussion about idol food; the question is never only about what I am permitted but what serves the other.
10.25: Eat whatever is sold in the meat market without raising questions of conscience — practical guidance for the agora; meat of unknown cultic origin does not require investigation. The earth is the Lord's and everything in it (Psalm 24:1).
10.26: Psalm 24:1 citation: the earth is the Lord's and the fullness thereof — all creation belongs to God; food purchased in the market is God's provision regardless of its prior use.
10.27: If an unbeliever invites you to dinner and you wish to go, eat whatever is set before you without asking questions of conscience — the same freedom applies at private dinners; don't interrogate the host about the food's provenance.
10.28: But if someone says this was offered in sacrifice (hierothyton is the pagan term; Paul used eidōlothyton, idol-meat, in chapter 8, using the insider critical term), do not eat it for the sake of the one who informed you and for the sake of conscience.
10.29: Paul means not your own conscience but the other's — the conscience at stake is not the knowledgeable believer's but the weaker person's or the unbeliever who raised the issue. Why should my freedom be judged by another's conscience?
10.30: If I partake with thankfulness, why am I slandered over that for which I give thanks? — another rhetorical question: if Paul eats with gratitude, the food is sanctified by prayer; but the point is that neighbor-conscience overrides personal thanksgiving-freedom in borderline cases.
10.31: So whether you eat or drink or whatever you do, do all to the glory of God — the summary principle; every act is subsumed under the telos of divine glory.
10.32: Give no offense to Jews or to Greeks or to the church of God — three communities whose stumbling must be avoided; the church is distinguished from both ethnic groups as a third category.
10.33: Paul himself tries to please all people in all things, not seeking his own advantage but that of the many, so that they may be saved — the apostle's own practice of self-limitation is the model that summarizes chapters 8-10 as a whole.

5.1: porneia (porneia) is Paul's broad term for sexual immorality, but the clause specifies a named species: a man has his father's wife (tēn gynaika tou patros echein). This is not adultery in the technical sense (the father may be dead or divorced) but incest-adjacent — a step-relationship prohibited explicitly in Lev 18:8 and Deut 22:30, with the death penalty at Lev 20:11. Paul's rhetorical holos akouetai (it is actually reported) signals the report has reached him in Ephesus and he treats it as established fact, not rumor.
5.2: pephysiōmenoi este (pephysiōmenoi este) — perfect passive: you are in a state of being puffed up. The same root (physioō) recurs in 4:6, 4:18, 4:19, 5:2, 8:1, 13:4 — it is one of Paul's recurring diagnoses of the Corinthian community's fundamental problem. penthēsēte (mourn) — the mourning vocabulary is that of lament for the dead or for communal sin (Jas 4:9; 2 Cor 12:21), not merely sorrow. exarthē (put out/removed) — aorist passive imperative: an action that should have already happened.
5.3: apōn tō sōmati, parōn de tō pneumati (apōn … parōn): absent in body, present in spirit. Paul asserts a real apostolic presence through the Spirit that enables genuine judicial action at a distance. kekrika (I have already passed judgment) — perfect indicative: the verdict is rendered, not deliberated. Paul's authority is not contingent on physical presence; his judgment in this matter precedes the assembly's action.
5.4: en tō onomati tou Kyriou Iēsou (in the name of the Lord Jesus) — the assembly is constituted by this invocation; the name makes Christ present. synachthentōn hymōn (when you are assembled) — the gathered community, not the individual, is the locus of this disciplinary action. The dynamis (power) of the Lord Jesus mentioned here is the same power invoked at baptism and exorcism — the action is charismatic, not merely procedural.
5.5: paradounai ton toiouton tō Satana (hand this man over to Satan): a formal judicial act of excommunication. Expulsion from the covenant community was understood in Second Temple Judaism to place one outside divine protection — in the domain where the Adversary operates (cf. Job 1–2; Luke 22:31). olethros tēs sarkos — the destruction of the sarx. The translation note flags this as contested: sarx here is not merely the physical body but the whole person as constituted under sin's power — the corruptible, mortal, sin-dominated self. The destruction of the sarx through suffering and exclusion serves the salvation of the pneuma — the person's restored spiritual standing before God on the Day of the Lord. This is corrective, not punitive in a final sense.
5.6: to kauchēma hymōn (your boasting) — not boasting about this specific case but the community's general self-congratulation (4:7: what do you have that you did not receive?). zymē (zymē, leaven): Paul deploys the Passover preparation metaphor. In Jewish practice, before Passover all leaven (chametz) was removed from the house — a thorough, house-by-house search. A small amount of yeast permeates the entire batch (holon to phyrama) — the metaphor applies directly: one unaddressed sin corrupts the whole community's moral integrity.
5.7: ekkatharate tēn palaian zymēn (purge out the old leaven) — aorist imperative, urgent action. azymoi este (you are unleavened) — present indicative: the indicative precedes the imperative; you are already what you are called to become. to pascha hēmōn etythē Christos (Christ our Passover lamb has been sacrificed) — Paul's explicit Passover typology: Christ is the pascha, the Passover sacrifice. The aorist etythē (was slaughtered/sacrificed) uses the technical Passover slaughter term. This is not an analogy but a typological fulfillment claim: the Exodus Passover was always moving toward this event.
5.8: heortazōmen (let us keep the festival) — present subjunctive of exhortation: the whole Christian life is a Passover festival begun by Christ's sacrifice. palaia zymē (old leaven) = the pre-conversion life characterized by kakias kai ponērias (malice and wickedness). azymois eilikrinias kai alētheias (unleavened bread of sincerity and truth) — the two nouns in apposition interpret what genuine post-Passover life looks like: eilikrineia (purity, unmixed quality, from sun-testing for impurities) and alētheia (truth, reality as opposed to pretense). The festival metaphor reframes ethics as cultic participation.
5.9: egrapsa hymin (I wrote to you) — aorist, referring to a prior letter now lost. This is the earliest evidence that Paul wrote to Corinth before 1 Corinthians — what some scholars call the Vorbrief. Paul corrects a misreading of that letter: they apparently interpreted his instruction about not associating with the sexually immoral as applying to outsiders, which he now clarifies was absurd.
5.10: epei ara (since otherwise) — a reductio ad absurdum: if the prohibition applied to all immoral people in the world, leaving the world would be the only option. Paul's instruction was never a call to social withdrawal but to internal community discipline. The list — sexually immoral, greedy, swindlers, idolaters — is the standard Pauline vice catalogue applied to outsiders about whom he makes no disciplinary claim.
5.11: nun de egrapsa hymin (but now I write to you) — the contrast with v. 9's prior letter is explicit: this letter clarifies. The clause adelphos onomazomenos (one who bears the name brother) is key — the disciplinary authority extends only within the named community. mēde synesthiein (not even to eat with such a person) — table fellowship in the ancient world was covenant-expression; refusing the shared meal is the practical enactment of exclusion from the covenant community.
5.12: ti gar moi tous exō krinein (what business is it of mine to judge those outside?) — rhetorical question. Paul draws a firm epistemological boundary: the church has no judicial authority over those outside it. God will judge them (tous de exō ho theos krinei, v. 13). But the inside is another matter: tous de esō hymeis krinete (those inside you judge). The community's disciplinary authority is internal and self-referential.
5.13: tous de exō ho theos krinei (God will judge those outside) — Paul attributes final judgment of outsiders to God alone, not to the community. exarate ton ponēron ex hymōn autōn — direct quotation of Deut 17:7 (also 19:19; 21:21; 22:21 — the refrain of the Deuteronomic purity-law): expel the wicked person from among you. Paul applies Torah's community-boundary law to the church. The aorist imperative is decisive and urgent.

7.1: peri de hōn egrapsate (now about the matters you wrote about) — the first of seven such formula-openings in 1 Corinthians (7:1; 7:25; 8:1; 12:1; 16:1; 16:12), signaling Paul's shift to answering the Corinthians' own questions. kalon anthrōpō gynaikos mē haptesthai (it is good for a man not to touch a woman) — another Corinthian slogan Paul quotes. Haptomai (touch) is a standard euphemism for sexual intercourse (cf. Gen 20:6 LXX). Paul neither endorses nor condemns the slogan — he immediately qualifies it with the concessive dia de tas porneias (but because of sexual immorality cases…).
7.2: dia de tas porneias (but because of sexual immorality cases) — the plural porneias implies recurring instances in the community. heautou gynaika/idion andra (his own wife / her own husband) — the reciprocal possession language is striking: each has their own spouse; neither spouse is communal property. This grammatical reciprocity anticipates the full mutual-authority language of v. 4.
7.3: tēn opheilomenēn eunonian apodidotō (let him fulfill the conjugal duty) — opheilomenēn (owed, due): marriage creates a debt structure; each spouse owes the other conjugal attention. Apodidōmi (give back, render) is the debt-payment verb — not gift language but obligation language. Paul frames marital sexual relations as a mutual duty, not a concession. This is among Paul's most egalitarian statements in a context where husbands routinely denied their wives access to their bodies.
7.4: hē gynē tou idiou sōmatos ouk exousiazei alla ho anēr; homoiōs de kai ho anēr — the mutual exousiazō (has authority over) is remarkable: each spouse has authority over the other's body, not merely their own. The statement is symmetrical (homoiōs de kai) — husband and wife are placed on identical terms regarding conjugal authority. In the context of Greco-Roman household ethics, where the wife was subordinate in all matters, this mutual claim on bodily access is counter-cultural.
7.5: mē apostereite allēlous (do not deprive each other) — apostereō (defraud, deprive) appears also at 6:8; here in the sexual context of mutual conjugal obligation. ei mē an ek symphōnou (except by mutual agreement) — the exception clause: abstinence is legitimate only when both agree. pros ton kairon (for a season) — temporary, bounded. scholasēte tē proseuchē (devote yourselves to prayer) — the purpose: ascetic abstinence for focused prayer, a recognized Jewish practice (cf. Joel 2:16; LXX 1 Sam 21:5). akrasia (lack of self-control) — Paul acknowledges desire as a real and potentially dangerous force.
7.6: touto de legō kata syngnōmēn, ou kat' epitagēn (I say this as a concession, not as a command) — Paul carefully distinguishes: what he says about the concession for abstinence (v. 5) is a syngnōmē (concession, permission), not a epitagē (divine command). This is an important hermeneutical marker: he is making a pastoral accommodation, not a theological absolute.
7.7: thelō de pantas anthrōpous einai hōs kai emauton (I wish that all people were like me) — Paul situates himself as a model of celibate life. The translation note flags the dispute: Paul's charisma (spiritual gift, v. 7b) for celibacy is a genuine gift (charisma is the standard Pauline term for Spirit-given capacity, cf. ch. 12), not a universal calling. hekastos idion charisma echei ek Theou, ho men houtōs, ho de houtōs (each has their own gift from God, one of one kind and another of another) — both marriage and celibacy are charismata; neither is superior in the sense that both are divine gifts.
7.8: tois agamois kai tais chērais (to the unmarried and to widows) — agamos (unmarried) is Paul's coinage for those who have never been married or who are no longer married; it appears only in this chapter. The recommendation meinai hōs kagō (to remain as I am) implies Paul himself is unmarried at this point — widowed (if previously married) or never married.
7.9: ei de ouk enkrateuontai, gamēsatōsan (but if they do not have self-control, let them marry) — enkrateuomai (have self-control, contain oneself) is the virtue of self-mastery prized in both Stoic ethics and Jewish piety. kreitton gar estin gamēsai ē pyrousthai (it is better to marry than to burn with passion) — pyrousthai (to burn, be inflamed): the metaphor of sexual desire as fire. Paul does not frame marriage as a necessary evil but as the appropriate remedy — kreitton (better), a comparative that implies both options have merit.
7.10: tois de gegamēkosi parangellō, ouk egō alla ho Kyrios (to the married I give this instruction — not from me but from the Lord) — Paul distinguishes sharply between his own judgment and a direct command from the Lord Jesus. Here he has an explicit saying of Jesus (cf. Mark 10:11-12; Matt 5:32): the wife (gynaika) must not separate (chōristhēnai) from her husband. Parangellō (command) is the authoritative instructional verb, stronger than legō (say) or gnōmēn didōmi (give my opinion, v. 25).
7.11: ean de kai chōristhē (but if she does separate) — Paul acknowledges that the separation may happen even against the command. His instruction in that case: remain unmarried (agamos menetō) or be reconciled (katallagētō) to her husband. Katallagē is Paul's reconciliation vocabulary (2 Cor 5:18-20; Rom 5:11) — here applied to marital breakdown. The possibility of reconciliation is kept open; permanent new remarriage is not authorized while the husband lives.
7.12: tois de loipois legō egō, ouch ho Kyrios (to the others I say — I, not the Lord) — Paul has no recorded saying of Jesus for this situation (mixed marriage). He explicitly flags this: his instruction carries apostolic authority but not a dominical command. Legō egō (I say, I) — the emphatic pronoun marks his own authority, distinct from the Lord's. The situation he addresses is post-conversion mixed marriage, not a believer marrying an unbeliever.
7.13: kai gynē ētis echei andra aiston (and a woman who has an unbelieving husband) — the symmetry: the situation applies equally to believing wife with unbelieving husband. mē aphietō ton andra (she should not divorce her husband) — the verb aphiēmi is the standard Greek divorce term. Paul's instruction is the same in both directions: do not initiate divorce from an unbelieving but consenting spouse.
7.14: hēgiastai gar ho anēr ho apistos en tē gynaiki (the unbelieving husband is made holy through his believing wife) — hēgiastai is perfect passive: a settled state of being-made-holy. This does not mean the unbelieving spouse is saved but that the household is brought within the sphere of sanctification by the presence of a believing partner. The translation note flags the dispute. Hagia (holy) applied to the children of such a union — the household participates in holiness by association.
7.15: ei de ho apistos chōrizetai, chōrizesthō (but if the unbelieving partner separates, let them go) — the exception: if the unbeliever initiates departure, the believer is not obligated to prevent it. ou dedoulōtai ho adelphos ē hē adelphē en tois toioutois (a brother or sister is not enslaved in such cases) — dedoulōtai (enslaved, bound) picks up the slavery metaphor that runs through ch. 7: marriage creates a binding, but the unbeliever's departure releases the binding. en eirēnē keklēken hēmas ho Theos (God has called us to peace) — the calling to peace overrides the compulsion to preserve a marriage that the unbeliever has already abandoned.
7.16: ti gar oidas, gynai, ei ton andra sōseis (for how do you know, wife, whether you will save your husband?) — the rhetorical question is a pastoral caution: do not stay in a destructive marriage on the grounds of the missionary hope, since that outcome is uncertain. The verse has been read optimistically (you might save them — so stay) or pessimistically (you might not save them — so don't chain yourself to that hope). The latter reading fits the context better: Paul is warning against treating the mixed marriage as a guaranteed mission field.
7.17: hōs ekklēsen ho Theos hekaston, houtōs peripatatō (let each person live as God has assigned them, as the Lord has called them) — the calling principle: the condition in which God's call found you is not a problem to be escaped but a vocation to be lived. Keklēken (has called) is the perfect: the call was made and remains in force. kai houtōs en tais ekklesiais pasais diatassō (this is my rule in all the churches) — Paul claims this as a universal apostolic teaching, not a local accommodation.
7.18: en peritomē eklēthē tis (was someone called while circumcised?) — the calling principle applied to Jewish identity. Epispasthō (let him not remove marks of circumcision) — epispasm: a surgical procedure used by some Jews to restore the appearance of foreskin, documented in 1 Macc 1:15. Paul forbids it: the marker of the prior covenant-identity need not be hidden. Similarly, the uncircumcised should not seek circumcision.
7.19: hē peritomē ouden estin kai hē akrobystia ouden estin, alla tērēsis entolōn Theou (circumcision is nothing and uncircumcision is nothing; keeping God's commands is what matters) — the radical relativization of the covenant marker: neither circumcision nor its absence has intrinsic value. What matters is entolōn tērēsis (keeping God's commands). This is not antinomian (Paul upholds commands) but anti-ethnic-marker: ethnic-covenant status does not constitute righteousness.
7.20: hekastos en tē klēsei hē eklēthē, en tautē menetō (let each person remain in the situation they were in when God called them) — the governing principle of vv. 17-24 stated plainly. Klēsis (calling, vocation): Paul may be using this in its sociological sense (condition: slave, free, circumcised, uncircumcised) or theological sense (divine call into Christ). Both are probably in view: God's call finds you in a particular condition and makes that condition the site of your vocation.
7.21: doulos eklēthēs; mē soi meletō (were you a slave when called? do not let it trouble you) — the advice is: your slave status does not impede your standing before God. all' ei kai dynasthai eleuthos genesthai, mallon chrēsai (but if you are indeed able to gain your freedom, take the opportunity) — the contested phrase. Mallon chrēsai (use/avail yourself more rather): grammatically ambiguous — take the freedom, or use your slave status (for the Lord). The majority of scholars now favor the manumission reading: if freedom is available, take it. Dynasthai (be able) implies an actual offer, not a hypothetical.
7.22: ho gar en Kyriō klētheis doulos apeleutheros Kyriou estin (the one who was a slave when called in the Lord is the Lord's freed person) — Paul applies the manumission metaphor to spiritual status: the slave who belongs to Christ has been set free (apeleutheros, freed person) in the most important sense. homoiōs ho eleutheros klētheis doulos Christou estin (similarly, the free person who was called is Christ's slave) — the free person, by coming to Christ, becomes a slave of Christ. The social asymmetry is inverted: freedom in Christ transcends and relativizes freedom in the world.
7.23: timēs ēgorasthēte; mē ginesthe douloi anthrōpōn (you were bought at a price; do not become slaves of people) — the same redemption-purchase formula as 6:20, now applied to social ethics: the purchased people of Christ must not trade their spiritual freedom for new social entanglements that compromise their primary allegiance. This is not a command to reject all social hierarchy but a warning against the patron-client dependencies that could compromise devotion to Christ.
7.24: hekastos en hō eklēthē, adelphoi, en toutō menetō para Theō (brothers and sisters, each of you should remain before God in the situation you were in when called) — the principle restated a third time, now with the addition para Theō (before God / with God). The presence of God reframes the social location: whatever your condition, you are standing before God in it. Translation note flags a dispute on para Theō: with God (in relationship) or before God (in God's sight).
7.25: peri de tōn parthenōn epitagēn Kyriou ouk echō, gnōmēn de didōmi (now about those who have never been married: I have no command from the Lord, but I give my judgment) — Paul is transparent about his epistemic status. Parthenōn (virgins, those who have never married) is the subject; he has no dominical tradition for this case. Gnōmēn didōmi (I give my judgment/opinion) — a weaker speech-act than parangellō (command) or legō without qualification. Pistos einai hypo Kyriou (to be trustworthy by the Lord's mercy) — his authority is mercy-given, not self-generated.
7.26: enestōsan anankēn (the present crisis) — anankē (necessity, crisis, compulsion): scholars debate what this refers to — persecution, famine, the general eschatological urgency of the age, or a local Corinthian situation. The word elsewhere in Paul (Rom 8:35; 2 Cor 12:10) describes tribulation. Enestōsan (present, standing near): the crisis is already upon them. In view of this urgency, remaining in one's current condition is tactically wise.
7.27: dedesai gynaiki; mē zētei lysin (are you committed to a wife? do not seek release) — dedesai (have been bound) is the perfect passive of the same verb used for legal binding. The symmetry of the verse is complete: the bound should not seek release; the free should not seek binding. Both options have equal standing; neither is commanded.
7.28: thlipsin de tē sarki hexousin hoi toioutoi (those who marry will face practical hardships) — thlipsis (tribulation, pressure): Paul is not saying marriage is sinful but that it introduces real-world complications in a time of eschatological pressure. Tē sarki (in the flesh): the hardships are material and embodied, not merely spiritual. Translation note flags sarx here as neutral (embodied life), not the ethical sense.
7.29: ho kairos synestalmenos estin (the time is contracted/compressed) — synestalmenos is a nautical-textile metaphor: a sail gathered in, a garment rolled up. Not merely short but pulled tight, urgent, wound up. The phrase anchors the whole of vv. 29-31: the compressed urgency of the eschatological moment means existing social structures should be worn lightly. Those with wives should live hōs mē echontes (as though not having them) — not abandoning them but not being absorbed by the relationship.
7.30: The fourfold hōs mē (as though not) series — mourning/not mourning, rejoicing/not rejoicing, buying/not owning — is Paul's eschatological detachment ethic. The present-age structures (grief, joy, commerce) are real but temporary. Hōs mē does not counsel withdrawal from these realities but a held-loosely engagement that refuses ultimate investment in them.
7.31: to schēma tou kosmou toutou paragetai (the present form of this world is passing away) — schēma (form, fashion, outward shape): not the world itself but its present configuration. Paragetai (is passing away) — present tense: the transition is already underway, not merely anticipated. Paul's eschatology is inaugurated: the age to come has begun its incursion into the present age through Christ's resurrection.
7.32: ho agamos merimnā ta tou Kyriou, pōs areskē tō Kyriō (the unmarried man is concerned about the things of the Lord — how to please the Lord) — merimnā (is anxious/concerned for): in Phil 4:6 Paul forbids anxiety; here he commends it, when it is directed toward the Lord. The point is undivided attention. Areskō (please): the goal of the celibate life is pleasing the Lord; the goal of the married life is also pleasing, but now divided between Lord and spouse.
7.33: ho de gamēsas merimnā ta tou kosmou, pōs areskē tē gynaiki (the married man is concerned about worldly affairs — how to please his wife) — Paul does not condemn this; pleasing one's spouse is a legitimate and good concern. But the structural division (memeristai, v. 34: is divided) is real. The celibate is not morally superior but has a structural simplicity in orientation.
7.34: memeristai (is divided) — perfect passive: a state of ongoing dividedness. hē agamos kai hē parthenos merimnā ta tou Kyriou (the unmarried woman or virgin is concerned about the Lord's things) — the symmetry with the male situation (vv. 32-33) is maintained: the unmarried woman has the same structural advantage as the unmarried man. Hagia kai tō sōmati kai tō pneumati (holy in body and spirit) — the celibate life is a whole-person holiness, not merely spiritual.
7.35: pros to hymōn sympheron legō (I say this for your own benefit) — Paul frames his preference for celibacy as pastoral care, not imposition. ouch hina brochos hymin epibalō (not to restrict you, literally not to throw a noose on you) — the metaphor of a restraint or snare: Paul denies that he is trying to limit freedom. pros to euschēmon kai euparedron tō Kyriō aperspastōs (to promote what is fitting and to enable undivided devotion to the Lord) — euparedron (sitting well beside, devoted attendance): a rare term meaning devoted proximity, continuous attendance. Undivided devotion (aperispastōs) is the goal.
7.36: ei de tis aschēmonein epi tēn parthenon autou nomizei (but if anyone thinks he is acting dishonorably toward his betrothed virgin) — the situation: a man betrothed to a virgin, constrained by the general principle of v. 26 (remain as you are), but now under social pressure that his restraint dishonors her. Hyperakmos (past her prime, beyond the peak of marriageable age): implies the woman is aging without marriage. Paul's pastoral response: if the man feels this obligation, let them marry — he does not sin.
7.37: hos de hestēken en tē kardia autou hedraios (but the man who stands firm in his commitment) — hedraios (settled, immovable, like a foundation): the architectural metaphor of stability. Three conditions: firm in commitment (hedraios), under no compulsion (mē echōn anankēn), desire under his own authority (exousian peri tou idiou thelēmatos). The man who freely chooses to keep his betrothed as his betrothed does well — the choice is genuinely free and honored.
7.38: hōste kai ho gamizōn tēn heautou parthenon kalōs poiei, kai ho mē gamizōn kreitton poiēsei (so the one who marries his betrothed does well, and the one who refrains from marrying does even better) — both options are kalōs (well); only the celibate option is kreitton (better). The superlative is comparative, not absolute: both are good; one has a contextual advantage. Paul will not forbid marriage; he will not pretend the advantage does not exist.
7.39: gynē dedetal hō chronō ean zē ho anēr autēs (a wife is bound to her husband as long as he lives) — dedetal (is bound): the same binding-verb as v. 27. Death dissolves the marital bond. Translation note flags nomos: the law by which she is bound may be Torah-law or the general principle. monon en Kyriō (only in the Lord) — the restriction on remarriage after the husband's death: the new marriage must be within the community of faith.
7.40: makariotēra de estin ean houtōs meinē (she will be happier if she remains as she is) — makariotēra: comparative of makarios (blessed), the beatitude-word. Paul's final opinion: the widow who remains unmarried is more blessed. dokō de kagō pneuma Theou echein (and I think I also have the Spirit of God) — a dry, careful claim: dokō (I think, I believe): not asserting certainty but claiming the same Spirit-guidance he has appealed to throughout. The modest self-defense implies someone in Corinth disputed whether Paul spoke with spiritual authority on these matters.

12.1: peri de tōn pneumatikōn (peri de tōn pneumatikōn): the phrase is grammatically ambiguous — the adjective pneumatikōn can be neuter plural ('spiritual things/gifts') or masculine plural ('spiritual persons'). The neuter reading suits the context better: Paul is addressing what spiritual gifts are and how they function. Ou thelō hymas agnoein (I do not want you to be ignorant) — a Pauline disclosure formula that signals an authoritative statement follows.
12.2: ethnē ete pros ta eidōla ta aphōna hōs an ēgesthe apagomenoi: 'you were Gentiles, led away to mute idols however you were led.' Aphōna (mute/voiceless) ironically contrasts with the Spirit-speech Paul is about to discuss — idols cannot speak, the Spirit does. Apagomenoi (led away) is a passive participle of compulsion: the pagan past was characterized by being driven rather than led by God's Spirit.
12.3: anathema Iēsous vs. kyrios Iēsous: the two confessional poles. Anathema = devoted to destruction, an LXX technical term for objects of divine curse (Deut 7:26). Whether 'Jesus be cursed' was a formula forced on believers under persecution or a Corinthian spiritual-pride claim ('the earthly Jesus is irrelevant, only the heavenly Christ matters') is debated. Either way, Paul's criterion is clear: the Spirit of God produces the confession of Jesus's lordship, never his cursing.
12.4: diaireseis charismaton (diaireseis charismaton): diairesis = distribution or distinction/variety — the same Spirit distributes various gifts rather than one gift to all. Charisma (grace-gift, plural charismata) is Paul's own coinage built on charis (grace) — a gift whose basis is God's favor, not human merit or capacity. The three-verse structure (vv. 4-6) attributes the variety of gifts, services, and workings to the Spirit, Lord, and God respectively — an early Trinitarian-patterned statement.
12.5: diaireseis diakoniōn (diaireseis diakoniōn): diakonia = service, ministry — the same word used for table-waiting (Acts 6:2) and apostolic ministry (2 Cor 6:3). Paul deliberately places 'service' as the second category of Spirit-distribution after 'gifts' — the charismata are not status-conferring trophies but service-functions within the community. Ho autos kyrios (the same Lord) — Christ is the source of the variety of ministry-forms.
12.6: energēmata (energēmata): workings, operations — from energeō (to be at work, cf. English 'energy'). The third category: active divine workings that express the gifts and services. Ho de autos Theos ho energōn ta panta en pasin (the same God who works all things in all people) — the sovereignty of God over all pneumatic activity: what looks like human charismatic diversity is the unified working of one God through many persons.
12.7: phanerōsis tou pneumatos (phanerōsis tou pneumatos): manifestation of the Spirit — phanerōō = to make visible/apparent. The individual gift is the Spirit's self-disclosure through the person; the gift is not owned by the receiver but is the Spirit appearing through them. Pros to sympheron (for the common good) — sympheron = advantage, benefit (same word as 6:12 and 10:23); the criterion for evaluating any exercise of a gift is its communal benefit.
12.8: logos sophias / logos gnōseōs: 'utterance of wisdom / utterance of knowledge.' Both are logos-compounds: not wisdom or knowledge as possession but as speech-act, oral communication. In the Corinthian context where sophia (wisdom) was the premium currency (1:17-2:5), Paul democratizes it: wisdom comes as a Spirit-given utterance to specific persons for specific occasions, not as the general intellectual achievement of the sophos.
12.9: pistis (faith, v. 9): distinct from saving faith — this is a special gift of faith for miraculous action (cf. 13:2: 'faith to move mountains'). Charismata iamatōn (gifts of healing): the plural of both charisma and iama (healing) is unusual — perhaps indicating multiple types of healing gifts or multiple instances, each a distinct grace-gift rather than a permanent endowment.
12.10: energēmata dynameōn (workings of powers/miracles): the dynamis vocabulary for miraculous acts; diakrisis pneumatōn (discernment of spirits): diakrisis = judgment, distinguishing — evaluating which spirits/prophecies are genuinely from God (cf. 1 John 4:1). Genē glōssōn (kinds of tongues): genē = varieties/kinds, suggesting differentiation even within the tongue-gift. Hermēneia glōssōn (interpretation of tongues): the complementary gift enabling tongues to function as intelligible speech in the assembly.
12.11: to hen kai to auto pneuma (the one and the same Spirit): hen and auto together emphatically assert unity — not merely one of a kind but numerically identical across all manifestations. Diairoun (distributing): the active participle of diairō = to divide and distribute — echoing diairesis in vv. 4-6. Kath' hekastō (to each one individually): the Spirit's distribution is personal and intentional, not random.
12.12: houtōs kai ho Christos (so also Christ): the body-analogy's conclusion identifies the body not merely 'with the church' but with Christ himself — the body is Christ. This goes beyond illustration: the Corinthians' treatment of the body's members is their treatment of Christ's own body. The Christological intensity of the ecclesiology emerges here: harming the community is harming Christ (cf. 9:5: 'whom you are persecuting').
12.13: en heni pneumati eis hen sōma ebaptisthēmen (in one Spirit we were all baptized into one body): the preposition en is instrumental ('by') or locative ('in') — either the Spirit is the agent or the sphere of baptism. Eis hen sōma (into one body) — baptism is not individual spiritual transaction but corporate incorporation. Eite Ioudaioi eite Hellēnes eite douloi eite eleutheroi: the social binary pairs (ethnic, legal) are transcended by the Spirit's baptism — the body is constituted across the deepest social divisions of the Roman world.
12.14: to sōma ouk estin hen melos alla polla (the body is not one member but many): the premise for the following argument against gift-competition. The body's plurality is not a problem to overcome but the structural reality that makes the body a body. A one-member body is not a body at all — it is a monstrosity. Paul uses this anatomical truism to attack the Corinthian impulse to reduce the body's richness to a single valued gift (tongues).
12.15: ean eipen ho pous (if the foot should say): the personified body-parts speak — the device allows Paul to give the gift-competition argument a reductio ad absurdum form. Para touto ouk estin ek tou sōmatos (on account of that it does not belong to the body) — the self-exclusion logic is exposed as incoherent: no body-part decides its own body-membership. The foot cannot disqualify itself by comparing itself unfavorably to the hand.
12.16: ean eipen to ous (if the ear should say): the second personification, repeating the structure. The parallel body-part arguments (foot/hand, ear/eye) hit different audience members: those who feel excluded (foot/ear) and those who might exclude others (eye saying to the hand, v. 21). Both are anatomical absurdities that expose the absurdity of spiritual gift competition and dismissal.
12.17: ei holon to sōma ophthalmos (if the whole body were an eye): the reductio from the body-pluralism argument. If the Corinthians got what they apparently wanted — everyone with the same gift (tongues being the most visible) — the 'body' would be a grotesque monstrosity, not a functioning organism. Pou hē akoe (where would the hearing be?); pou hē osphrēsis (where would the sense of smell be?) — the anatomy drives the ecclesiology.
12.18: nun de ho Theos etheto ta melē hekastou autōn en tō sōmati kathōs ēthelésen (God has placed each member in the body as he chose): etheto (aorist of tithēmi, to place, appoint) — God's active arrangement of the body's diversity is a creation-level decision, not an accidental variation. Kathōs ēthelésen (as he chose) — the theological grounding: gift-distribution reflects divine sovereignty and intention, not spiritual achievement.
12.19: ei de ēn ta panta hen melos (if all were one member): the hypothetical homogeneity argument continued. Pou to sōma (where would the body be?) — the body would cease to exist as a body. The argument proves from anatomy that unity requires diversity: a body of identical members is not a body but a mass of undifferentiated tissue. The Corinthians' preference for uniformity (all speak in tongues) would, paradoxically, destroy the very body they belong to.
12.20: nun de polla men melē hen de sōma (many members but one body): the summary thesis of the body-analogy argument. The men/de construction (many... but one) holds the tension without collapsing either side. This is Paul's ecclesiological statement in its most compressed form: the community is one organism precisely because it is composed of diverse, non-interchangeable members. The unity is not uniformity.
12.21: ou dynatai ho ophthalmos eipein tē cheiri chreia sou ouk echō (the eye cannot say to the hand 'I have no need of you'): the argument from an opposing direction — now the strong member cannot exclude the seemingly weaker one. Ou dynatai (cannot) — the impossibility is anatomical and theological. The body's interdependence is a structural feature, not a moral aspiration. The eye's apparent independence from the hand is an illusion of function-specialization, not independence of existence.
12.22: ta dokounte asthenesteron hyparchein melē tou sōmatos anankaia estin (the members that seem weaker are indispensable): asthenesteron = weaker, more fragile (the comparative of asthenēs); anankaia = necessary, indispensable — the paradox that the apparently fragile members are the most necessary. This overturns the Corinthian honor-scale: the visible, dramatic gifts (tongues) are not more valuable than the apparently weaker ones (helps, administration).
12.23: ta atimōtera tou sōmatos toutois timēn perissoteran peritheumen (on these we bestow greater honor): atimotera (less honored, comparative of atimos) vs. perissoteran timēn (greater honor) — the honor-reversal: human practice actually reinforces the body's care for less-honored members by clothing them more carefully. Euschēmosynēn perissoteran echei (has greater modesty) — the anatomy of clothing practices demonstrates the principle Paul wants for the church.
12.24: ta de euschēmona hēmōn ou chreian echei (our presentable members have no need): euschēmona (well-formed, honorable) — the presentable, naturally dignified members need no additional honor. Synekrasen to sōma tō hysterountī perissoterēn dous timēn (God has blended the body together, giving greater honor to the lesser part): synkeraō = to mix together, blend — God is the one who deliberately constructs the body with this compensatory honor-distribution to the disadvantaged members.
12.25: hina mē ē schisma en tō sōmati (so that there may be no division in the body): schisma = tear, division — the same word used for the community divisions in 1:10 and 11:18. The body-analogy's purpose is anti-schismatic: understanding the body's unity-in-diversity is the cure for factional gift-competition. Merimnosōsin (care for one another) — merimnaō = to be anxious about, to take care of; the mutual concern is the positive expression of the body's unity.
12.26: ei pathei hen melos sympaschei panta ta melē (if one member suffers, all the members suffer together): sympaschō = to suffer together with (syn-compound) — the involuntary solidarity of a body that shares neural connection. Syndoxazetai panta ta melē (if one member is honored, all the members rejoice together) — syndoxazō = to be glorified together. The positive and negative solidarity are symmetrical: what happens to one happens to all. This is the theological basis for communal lament and communal celebration.
12.27: hymeis de este sōma Christou kai melē ek merous (you are the body of Christ and individually members of it): the direct ecclesiological claim, no longer analogy. Sōma Christou (body of Christ) — the community is identified as Christ's body, not merely organized like a body. Ek merous (each in his part/individually) — each person is a member, not the whole. The corporate identification with Christ is total; the individual's position within it is partial and defined by relation to the whole.
12.28: etheto ho Theos en tē ekklēsia prōton apostolous deuteron prophētas triton didaskalous (God has appointed in the church first apostles, second prophets, third teachers): prōton... deuteron... triton is a ranked sequence, then the list shifts to ungrouped items. Antilēmpseis (acts of helping) — a hapax in Paul, from antilambanō = to take hold, to help; the term may suggest supporting the weak. Kyberneseis (acts of guiding/administration) — from kybernaō, to pilot/steer a ship; used for governance and organizational leadership.
12.29: mē pantes apostoloi (are all apostles?): the rhetorical mē expects a negative answer — 'not all are apostles, are they?' The sequence of seven rhetorical questions (vv. 29-30) demolishes any claim that one gift is universally distributed. Mē pantes charismata echōsin iamatōn (do all have gifts of healing?) — the negative answer to each question is the argument against the Corinthian elevation of any single gift as the mark of genuine spirituality.
12.30: mē pantes glōssais lalousin (do all speak in tongues?): the question aimed directly at the Corinthian preference. The implied answer — 'of course not' — undermines the unstated claim that tongues is the spiritual gift that all genuine believers must manifest. Mē pantes diermēneuousin (do all interpret?) — the follow-up makes the point: the gift of tongues requires the paired gift of interpretation to be communally useful.
12.31: zēloute de ta charismata ta meizona (eagerly desire the greater gifts): zēloō = to be zealous for, to eagerly pursue — the same verb will appear in 14:1, 39. The 'greater' gifts are those most beneficial for the community (prophecy over tongues, because prophecy is intelligible). Kai eti kath' hyperbolēn hodon hymin deiknymi (and I show you a still more excellent way) — kath' hyperbolēn = beyond all comparison, superabundantly; the transition to the love-chapter. Love is not a gift but the way in which all gifts must operate.

14.1: diōkete tēn agapēn (pursue love): diōkō = to pursue, chase after — the same verb used for persecution, here applied to the active pursuit of love. The imperative is urgent and directional: love is not passively received but actively sought. Zēloute de ta pneumatika (eagerly desire spiritual gifts): zēloō = to be zealous, to burn with desire — the echo of 12:31 now contextualized within love as the operative mode. Mallon de hina prophēteuēte (especially that you may prophesy) — the preference for prophecy over tongues is the chapter's central argument.
14.2: ho gar lalōn glōssē ouk anthrōpois lalei alla Theō (the one speaking in a tongue speaks not to people but to God): the directional problem with tongues-without-interpretation — the communication is vertical (God-ward) but the assembly context requires horizontal (community-ward) intelligibility. Pneumati de lalei mystēria (but in the spirit he speaks mysteries): mystēria = hidden things, not yet intelligible; the tongue-speaker accesses divine reality but cannot convey it to others without interpretation.
14.3: ho de prophēteuōn anthrōpois lalei oikodoomen kai paraklēsin kai paramythian (the one who prophesies speaks upbuilding, encouragement, and consolation to people): three terms that together describe prophecy's communal function. Oikodomē = construction, building up — Paul's key criterion for all communal practice. Paraklēsis = encouragement, exhortation (the same root as Paraclete — the Spirit's own name). Paramythia = consolation, comfort — used here only in Paul; a distinctly pastoral function.
14.4: ho lalōn glōssē heauton oikodomeī (the one speaking in a tongue builds himself up): heauton = himself — the reflexive pronoun marks the gift's self-directed benefit. Ho de prophēteuōn ekklēsian oikodomeī (the one who prophesies builds up the church): the community-directed benefit. Paul is not denigrating self-edification but arguing from the assembly context: gathered worship is primarily for communal building, not individual spiritual enrichment. Both are real goods; in the communal setting, communal benefit takes priority.
14.5: thelō de pantas hymas lalein glōssais (I want all of you to speak in tongues): Paul's surprising affirmation — he is not opposed to tongues, he desires it universally. Mallon de hina prophēteuēte (but more that you prophesy) — the comparative mallon (more, rather) sets up the preference without absolute prohibition. Ektos ei mē diermēneuē (unless he interprets): the crucial condition that makes tongues communally equivalent to prophecy — interpretation renders them equivalent in function.
14.6: ti hymas ōphelēsō ean mē hymin lalēsō ē en apokalypsei ē en gnōsei ē en prophēteia ē en didachē (how will I benefit you unless I speak to you in revelation, knowledge, prophecy, or teaching?): ōpheleō = to benefit, profit — the utilitarian criterion is communal benefit. The four modes of intelligible speech (revelation, knowledge, prophecy, teaching) represent the range of Spirit-given communication that edifies; uninterpreted tongues falls outside all four categories for the assembly's purpose.
14.7: ta apycha phōnēn didonta (lifeless things giving forth sound): apychos = without soul/life (the compound of a-privative + psychē). Musical instruments — flute (aulos) and harp (kithara) — make sounds that require musical structure (intervals, melody) to be music rather than noise. Pōs gnōsthēsetai to auloumenon ē to kitharizomenon (how will what is played on the flute or harp be known?) — the musical analogy: undifferentiated sound is unintelligible regardless of the instrument's inherent quality.
14.8: ean gar adēlon salpinx phōnēn dō (for if the trumpet gives an indistinct sound): salpinx = battle trumpet; adēlon = unclear, uncertain — the negation of dēlos (evident/clear). In ancient warfare, trumpet calls had specific meanings (advance, retreat, rally) — an unclear call would produce confusion rather than coordinated action. Tis paraskeuasetai eis polemon (who will prepare for battle?) — the military analogy captures the life-or-death stakes of intelligible communication in the assembly context.
14.9: houtōs kai hymeis dia tēs glōssēs ean mē eusoēmon logon dōte (likewise with you, if through the tongue you do not give intelligible speech): eusoēmos = well-signaling, distinct, clear — eu (well) + sēma (sign/signal). Eis aera laleite (you are speaking into the air): the devastating summary — uninterpreted tongue-speech passes through the assembly space without landing anywhere. The air receives it; no human mind is addressed or built up. The image reinforces the directional critique of v. 2.
14.10: tosauta ei tychoi genē phōnōn estin en kosmō (there are perhaps so many kinds of sounds in the world): ei tychoi = as it happens, whatever the number may be — a casual epistemic hedge. Genē = kinds, varieties. Kai ouden aphōnon (and none of them is without meaning): aphōnos = voiceless, without meaningful sound — Paul's point is that all languages in the world, however foreign-sounding, have communicative structure for their speakers. The tongue-speaker's sounds are similarly structured for God but unintelligible to the assembly.
14.11: ean oun mē eidō tēn dynamin tēs phōnēs (if I do not know the meaning/force of the sound): dynamis tēs phōnēs = the power/force of the voice — the communicative capacity of a given speech-form. Barbaros tō lalounti (a foreigner to the speaker) — barbaros = one whose speech sounds like 'bar-bar' to Greek ears — the onomatopoeic term for unintelligible foreigner-speech. The mutual barbarian-status: both parties are unintelligible to each other when there is no shared linguistic code. The assembly context requires a shared code.
14.12: zēlōtai este pneumatōn (you are zealots for spiritual manifestations): zēlōtēs = one who burns with zeal (the political Zealots took this name for themselves). Paul acknowledges the Corinthian enthusiasm as real but misdirected. Pros tēn oikodomēn tēs ekklēsias zēteite hina perisseūēte (seek to abound for the upbuilding of the church) — the redirect: the same zeal should be directed toward gifts that build the community. Perisseúō = to abound, overflow — Paul wants the Corinthians' enthusiasm channeled toward communal benefit.
14.13: ho lalōn glōssē proseuchesthō hina diermēneuē (let the one speaking in a tongue pray that he may interpret): the gift-pairing requirement — the tongue-speaker should seek the complementary gift of interpretation. Diermēneuō = to interpret, translate (the dia-compound intensifies: to translate thoroughly). The prayer for the interpretive gift is itself an act of communal orientation: the tongue-speaker is learning to value the community's understanding as much as his own spiritual experience.
14.14: ean gar proseuchomai glōssē to pneuma mou proseuchetai ho de nous mou akarpos estin (for if I pray in a tongue my spirit prays but my mind is unfruitful): to pneuma mou = my spirit — the human spirit engaged in prayer. Ho de nous mou = but my mind (the rational faculty). Akarpos = unfruitful, without fruit — the mind produces no communicable understanding during tongue-prayer. The cognitive dimension of prayer (which shapes, forms, and communicates) is bypassed. Paul does not dismiss spirit-prayer but insists the mind must also be engaged.
14.15: proseuxomai tō pneumati proseuxomai de kai tō noi (I will pray with the spirit and I will pray with the mind also): the both-and resolution — neither spirit-prayer alone nor mind-prayer alone, but both together. Psallō tō pneumati psallō de kai tō noi (I will sing with the spirit and sing with the mind also) — the singing dimension: psallō = to sing psalms/hymns (originally to pluck a stringed instrument). The principle applies to all devotional acts: full engagement of spirit and mind together is the Pauline standard.
14.16: epi tō sō eucharistia (at your giving of thanks): eucharistia = thanksgiving — the term used for the eucharistic prayer and for table-blessings. Idiōtēs (the ungifted person/layperson): idiōtēs = a private person, one who does not hold a public role — in this context, one who does not have the gift of tongues or interpretation. Pōs erei to amēn (how will he say Amen?) — the congregational Amēn was the community's ratification of a prayer; inability to understand the prayer-content makes ratification impossible.
14.17: sy men gar kalōs eucharisteis all' ho heteros ouk oikodomeītai (for you give thanks well enough, but the other person is not being built up): the men/de (you... but the other) contrast is precise: Paul concedes the spiritual quality of the tongue-speaker's thanksgiving while insisting the communal building-up criterion is not met. Kalōs (well) — the tongue-prayer is genuinely good in itself; the problem is not its quality but its location and context.
14.18: eucharistō tō Theō pantōn hymōn mallon glōssais lalō (I thank God that I speak in tongues more than all of you): Paul's astonishing personal disclosure — his tongue-speaking exceeds even the Corinthians' practice. This is not modesty but strategic rhetoric: his argument against tongues in the assembly cannot be dismissed as the complaint of someone who lacks or devalues the gift. He has it; he surpasses them in it; and he still insists on intelligible speech in the gathering.
14.19: en ekklēsia thelō pente logous dia tou noos mou lalēsai (in the assembly I would rather speak five words with my mind): the dramatic ratio — five intelligible words vs. ten thousand in a tongue. Dia tou noos mou (through my mind) — mind-engaged speech, not bypassing rational faculties. Hina kai allous katēchēsō (so that I might instruct others also) — katēcheō = to instruct orally (origin of English 'catechism'); the oral-instruction model for assembly speech. Myrious logous en glōssē = ten thousand words in a tongue — the hyperbole underlines the inversion of Corinthian values.
14.20: mē paidia ginesthe tais phresin (do not be children in your thinking): phrenes = mind, practical reason — the cognitive faculties as a whole. Alla tē kakia nēpiazete (but in evil be infants): a paradox — Paul wants Corinthian immaturity reversed in cognition while recommending a childlike inexperience in evil. Tais phresin teleioi ginesthe (but in your thinking be mature): teleios = complete, mature — the same term as 13:10 and 2:6. Gift-competition and tongues-preference are symptoms of cognitive immaturity; the corrective is the mature mind that evaluates gifts by communal benefit.
14.21: en tō nomō gegraptai (it is written in the Law): the citation is from Isa 28:11-12, referred to as 'the Law' — Paul uses nomos broadly for all Scripture. The Isaiah text describes YHWH speaking to the northern kingdom en heteroglōssois (in foreign tongues/languages) — a sign of judgment addressed to those who refused to hear plain speech. Paul's typological argument: tongues function as a sign for unbelievers (in the Isaianic sense — a sign of judgment on the unbelieving), while prophecy functions for believers.
14.22: hōste hai glōssai eis sēmeion eisin ou tois pisteuousin alla tois apistois (so then tongues are a sign, not for believers but for unbelievers): sēmeion = sign — a significant action with referential meaning beyond itself. The Isaiah use of foreign-tongue speech was a judgment-sign to the unbelieving nation; tongues function analogously as a sign-act. Hē de prophēteia ou tois apistois alla tois pisteuousin (but prophecy is not for unbelievers but for believers) — the complementary assignment: each gift has a different primary audience and function.
14.23: ean oun synelthē hē ekklēsia holē epi to auto (if then the whole church comes together in one place): epi to auto = in one place, together — the full-assembly scenario. Pantes glōssais lalousin (all speak in tongues): the maximalist tongues-scenario produces the perception of madness. Mainesthe = you are out of your mind — the visitor's verdict uses the same term used for Paul's supposed madness before Festus (Acts 26:24). Unintelligible speech is indistinguishable from madness to the outsider.
14.24: ean de pantes prophēteuōsin (but if all prophesy): the contrasting scenario — universal prophecy in the assembly produces a different dynamic. Elenchetai hypo pantōn (he is convicted by all) — elenchō = to expose, convict, reprove (the Spirit's own work in John 16:8). Anakrinetai hypo pantōn (he is examined/discerned by all) — anakrinō = to examine carefully. The unbeliever who enters a prophesying assembly undergoes a Spirit-driven examination of his inner life.
14.25: ta krupta tēs kardias autou phanera ginetai (the secrets of his heart are made manifest): krupta = hidden, secret things (the inner life); phanera ginetai = become visible/evident — the prophetic word exposes what was concealed. The eschatological imagery: the Day of the Lord will manifest all hidden things (1 Cor 4:5); prophecy anticipates that disclosure in the present assembly. Pesōn epi prosōpon proskunēsei tō Theō (falling on his face he will worship God): the conversion described through the posture of submission — the involuntary prostration before divine reality.
14.26: hekastos psalmon echei, didachēn echei, apokalypsin echei, glōssan echei, hermēneian echei (each has a psalm, a teaching, a revelation, a tongue, an interpretation): the charismatic plurality of the Corinthian assembly — Paul describes it without dismissal. The five-item list represents the range of contributions members bring. Panta pros oikodomēn ginesthō (let all be done for building up) — the single criterion repeated: the communal building function is the test for any contribution.
14.27: eite glōssē tis lalei, kata duo ē to pleiston treis (if anyone speaks in a tongue, let it be two or at most three): the quantitative limit — not prohibition but regulation.
