Ana meros (each in turn) — sequential, not simultaneous; the orderly queue of speakers. Kai heis diermēneuetō (and let one person interpret): a single interpreter for all tongue-speakers in the session, ensuring coherence rather than competing interpretations.
14.28: ean de mē ē diermēneutēs, sigatō en ekklēsia (but if there is no interpreter, let him be silent in the assembly): sigatō = let him be silent — the same verb as in v. 34 (women's silence). The silence is conditional on the absence of interpretation, not absolute. Heautō de lale tō theō (let him speak to himself and to God) — the private exercise of the gift remains valid; the assembly context requires the interpretive pair.
14.29: prophētai de duo ē treis lalesatōsan kai hoi alloi diakrinetōsan (let two or three prophets speak and let the others weigh/discern): diakrinetōsan = let them discern/distinguish (aorist imperative of diakrino) — the community's testing of prophetic utterances. This is the diakrisis pneumatōn (discernment of spirits) of 12:10 exercised in the assembly setting. Prophecy is not self-validating; it requires communal evaluation.
14.30: ean de allō apokalythptē kathēmenō, ho prōtos sigatō (but if something is revealed to another sitting there, let the first be silent): the mechanics of ordered prophetic exchange. Apokalyptō = to reveal — the prophetic insight arrives to a seated listener during another's speech. The first speaker must yield: the gift does not override the will, and the prophet can control his speech. This directly counters any claim of overwhelming spiritual compulsion that cannot be regulated.
14.31: pantes gar dynasthe kath' hena prophēteuein, hina pantes manthanōsin kai pantes parakalōntai (for you can all prophesy one by one, so that all may learn and all may be encouraged): kath' hena = one by one, sequentially — the possibility of universal participation regulated by order. Manthanōsin (learn) — prophecy has a didactic function alongside its experiential one. Parakalōntai (be encouraged/exhorted) — the same root as paraklēsis in v. 3: prophecy's pastoral function recapitulated in the purpose clause.
14.32: kai pneumata prophētōn prophētais hypotassetai (and the spirits of prophets are subject to prophets): pneumata prophētōn = the spirits of prophets — either the human spirit of the prophets or the prophetic impulse. Hypotassō = to arrange under, subject to — the claim that the prophetic endowment does not override the prophet's volitional control. This refutes any 'I couldn't help myself' defense for disorderly behavior. The prophet remains the agent; the Spirit equips without coercing.
14.33: ou gar estin akatastasias ho Theos alla eirēnēs (for God is not a God of disorder but of peace): akatastasia = instability, disorder, tumult (used in Jas 3:16 alongside jealousy and selfish ambition). The theological argument from God's character: ordered assembly worship reflects the orderliness of God himself. Hōs en pasais tais ekklēsiais tōn hagiōn (as in all the churches of the saints) — the universality claim: Corinth's practice should conform to the pattern of all Paul's churches.
14.34: hai gynaikes en tais ekklēsiais sigatōsan (women should be silent in the assemblies): one of the most debated commands in the NT. The connection to the orderly-assembly context and the parallel with tongues-silence (v. 28) and prophets yielding (v. 30) suggests the silence is contextual (evaluating prophecy? disruptive questioning?) rather than absolute. Textual issues: some manuscripts place vv. 34-35 after v. 40, suggesting possible interpolation. The command may be directed at a specific Corinthian disruption pattern: wives questioning their husbands' prophesying in the assembly.
14.35: ei de ti mathein thelousin, en oikō tous idious andras eperotatōsan (if they want to learn something, let them ask their own husbands at home): eperotatōsan = let them ask/question — the verb is for interrogation, inquiry. En oikō = at home — the private domestic sphere as the appropriate venue for women's theological questions. Aischron gar estin gynaiki lalein en ekklēsia (for it is shameful for a woman to speak in the assembly) — aischron = shameful, disgraceful; the cultural shame-honor dynamic invoked for the assembly context.
14.36: ē aph' hymōn ho logos tou Theou exēlthen (or did the word of God originate from you?): the rhetorical rebuke addressed to a self-important community that thinks it can set its own norms. Ē eis hymas monous katēntēsen (or has it come to you alone?) — the double rhetorical question attacks Corinthian exceptionalism: they did not generate the gospel and they are not its sole possessors. Both questions are answered with an implied 'no', deflating any claim to authority over the practice of all the churches.
14.37: ei tis dokei prophētēs einai ē pneumatikos, epiginsketō ha graphō hymin hoti kyriou estin entolē (if anyone thinks he is a prophet or spiritual, let him acknowledge that what I write to you is a command of the Lord): the test of genuine spiritual maturity is recognition of apostolic authority. Dokei = considers himself, seems to himself — the self-evaluation. Entolē kyriou (a command of the Lord) — Paul's instructions carry dominical authority. The irony: the truly Spirit-filled prophet recognizes the Spirit speaking through the apostle.
14.38: ei de tis agnoei, agnoeitai (but if anyone ignores this, he is ignored): agnoeō = to be ignorant of, to ignore — the double use creates a lex talionis structure: the one who ignores (the apostolic command) is himself ignored (by God). The passive agnoeitai may be a divine passive: God does not recognize the one who refuses to recognize the Spirit's ordering of the community. The eschatological stakes of the communal-order discussion are thereby revealed.
14.39: hōste adelphoi, zēloute to prophēteuein kai to lalein glōssais mē kōlyete (so then, brothers, eagerly desire to prophesy and do not forbid speaking in tongues): the balanced conclusion. Zēloute = eagerly desire (the imperative of the verb used positively throughout the chapter for the right kind of gift-seeking). Mē kōlyete = do not forbid — kōlyō = to prevent, hinder. The two prohibitions/permissions are carefully balanced: prioritize prophecy, but do not suppress tongues. Paul's pastoral wisdom avoids both extremes: tongues-mania and tongues-prohibition.
14.40: panta de euschēmonōs kai kata taxin ginesthō (but let all things be done properly and in order): euschēmonōs = properly, becomingly, fittingly — from euschēmōn (well-formed, honorable, cf. 12:24). Kata taxin = according to order — taxis is the military/civic ordering term. The summary principle governs all that has come before in chs. 12-14: gift diversity, love's primacy, and orderly assembly practice all derive from God's own character (v. 33: 'God is not a God of disorder'). The ordered assembly is itself a theological statement about the God who is worshipped.

16.1: Now concerning the collection for the saints (hē logia eis tous hagious) — logia is a technical term for a charitable monetary collection; this is the Jerusalem relief fund Paul organized throughout the Gentile churches (Rom 15:25-27; 2 Cor 8-9; Gal 2:10). He gives the same directive he gave the churches of Galatia.
16.2: On the first day of every week each of you is to put something aside — the first day (Sunday, resurrection day) becomes the regular occasion for communal financial planning; the weekly rhythm ties giving to the resurrection calendar.
16.3: When I arrive, I will send those whom you accredit by letter to carry your gift to Jerusalem — Paul does not handle the money himself; he appoints delegates to carry it, guarding against any accusation of financial impropriety.
16.4: If it seems advisable that I should go also, they will accompany me — Paul leaves the decision open; his journey to Jerusalem with the collection becomes the fateful trip described in Acts 21.
16.5: I will visit you after passing through Macedonia — Paul's intended travel route: from Ephesus through Macedonia then to Corinth. The letter was written from Ephesus (v.8).
16.6: I intend to stay with you or even spend the winter so that you may help me on my journey wherever I go — the language of being sent on by the congregation (propemphthō, to be provided with provisions for travel) was standard in early Christian hospitality.
16.7: I do not want to see you now just in passing; I hope to spend some time with you if the Lord permits — the qualification the Lord permits (ean ho kyrios epitrepsē) reflects Paul's consistent acknowledgment that apostolic plans are subject to divine direction (Acts 18:21; Jas 4:15).
16.8: But I will stay in Ephesus until Pentecost — Ephesus is the base of operations; Pentecost marks the festival calendar that Paul still observed.
16.9: A wide door for effective work has opened to me, and there are many adversaries — thyra megalē (great door) is a metaphor for missionary opportunity; the adversaries (antikeimenos, those opposing) confirm that the opportunity is contested.
16.10: If Timothy comes, see that he is with you without fear — Paul sends Timothy as his representative but is uncertain when he will arrive; the community is instructed to put him at ease, since he is doing the work of the Lord as I am.
16.11: Let no one despise him (exouthenēsē) — the same verb used of despising the weak in the body (12:22) and of judging Paul's presence as weak (2 Cor 10:10); Timothy is apparently young and unimposing and at risk of being dismissed.
16.12: As for our brother Apollos, I strongly urged him to visit you with the other brothers, but it was not at all his will to come now — Apollos (the Alexandrian teacher of Acts 18:24-28, around whom one Corinthian faction formed, 1:12) declines Paul's request; Paul presents this without criticism, respecting Apollos's own judgment.
16.13: Be watchful (grēgoreite), stand firm in the faith (stēkete), act like men (andrizesthe), be strong (krataioūsthe) — four military-style imperatives in rapid succession. The first three are standard battlefield commands; krataioūsthe (be strong) echoes Psalm 31:24 and Deuteronomy 31:6-7.
16.14: Let all that you do be done in love (en agapē) — after the four assertive imperatives, the fifth overarching command reframes all the others; force without agape is militarism, not Christian community.
16.15: The household of Stephanas was the first converts in Achaia (aparche tēs Achaias) — aparche (firstfruits) again, as in 15:20, 23; the first believers in a region are the firstfruits of that harvest.
16.16: Be subject to such as these and to everyone who works and toils with them — hypotassō (be subject) applied not to ordained office-holders but to those who labor (kopiaō) in the community's service; authority flows from faithful service.
16.17: I rejoice at the coming of Stephanas and Fortunatus and Achaicus, because they have made up for your absence — these three are apparently the bearers of the Corinthian letter to Paul (7:1) and have supplied what Paul missed in not having the Corinthians present.
16.18: They refreshed my spirit and yours — anapauō (refresh, give rest) is the word Jesus used in the invitation to rest in him (Matt 11:28); those who serve the apostle reflect Christ's own refreshing presence.
16.19: The churches of Asia send you greetings — plural: there were multiple congregations in the province of Asia by this point. Aquila and Prisca with the church in their house send you hearty greetings in the Lord — the husband-wife team who traveled with Paul (Acts 18:2, 18, 26) host a house-church.
16.20: All the brothers greet you. Greet one another with a holy kiss — the holy kiss (philēma hagion) appears in all of Paul's letter-closings where greetings are mentioned (Rom 16:16; 2 Cor 13:12; 1 Thess 5:26); it was the customary form of greeting elevated into a liturgical act of Christian communion.
16.21: I, Paul, write this greeting with my own hand — Paul typically dictated his letters and added a closing greeting in his own hand as authentication (Col 4:18; 2 Thess 3:17; Gal 6:11); the signature verified the letter's genuineness.
16.22: If anyone has no love for the Lord, let him be accursed (anathema) — the opposite of love for the Lord is subject to the covenant curse (anathema, Greek equivalent of the Hebrew herem). Maranatha — the Aramaic prayer preserved in Greek letters: marana tha (our Lord, come!) or maran atha (our Lord has come). Either way it is the earliest Aramaic liturgical expression in the NT, reflecting the prayer-language of the earliest Aramaic-speaking church.
16.23: The grace of the Lord Jesus be with you — the standard Pauline letter-closing benediction; charis (grace) is the last theological word before the personal farewell.
16.24: My love be with you all in Christ Jesus — an unusual personal closing; Paul rarely adds a statement of his own love. The letter that began with divisions and rebukes ends with Paul's personal affection extending to all, in Christ Jesus.
